technical_issue: The patch attempted to introduce a local modification to the meson build system to handle cross-compilation of Qt6 tools, but build-system related issues should be resolved via upstream-accepted patches rather than locally-maintained downstream patches.
corrective_action: Do not submit local workarounds for build-system toolchain detection; instead, identify or submit the necessary fix to the upstream meson project so it can be integrated into the official package version.
commit_message:
From: Quentin Schulz <quentin.schulz@cherry.de>
Qt tools that are looked by the meson qt module are only required as a
build time dependency, to be run on the Buildroot host machine.
Therefore, meson should be looking for them on the host machine and not
on the Buildroot target.
This fixes Buildroot meson not being able to find the qt6 tools whenever
necessary (e.g. for libcamera qcam, whose support is added in a later
commit).
Signed-off-by: Quentin Schulz <quentin.schulz@cherry.de>

corrective_action: Before submitting patches to remove or modify existing package patches, contributors must verify the current upstream version to ensure the issue has not already been resolved natively in newer releases.

technical_issue: The package was rejected due to its proposed location in the boot/ directory. Buildroot maintains a distinction between packages (user-space applications and libraries) and specialized boot-stage components. While some boot components reside in boot/, firmware blobs or low-level platform configuration utilities (like NXP's RCW or i.MX firmware) that function as dependencies for other bootloaders rather than as standalone boot targets often reside in the package/ directory, especially when they act as build-time dependencies.
corrective_action: The package should be moved from boot/ to the package/ directory to maintain consistency with existing similar NXP Layerscape infrastructure (e.g., qoriq-rcw). Contributors should prioritize placing non-bootloader, dependency-only firmware packages into package/ to avoid polluting the dedicated boot/ namespace reserved for primary bootloaders.

technical_issue: The contributor falsely labeled a hardware-specific Device Tree modification as 'Buildroot specific' simply to bypass the check-package script. Device Tree patches are inherently upstreamable to the Linux kernel and are not unique to Buildroot, therefore claiming they are 'Buildroot specific' is factually incorrect and misrepresents the origin and nature of the patch.
corrective_action: Do not use 'Upstream: Not applicable, Buildroot specific' for patches that modify kernel sources or hardware definitions. If a patch fixes a hardware issue, it should be submitted to the upstream Linux kernel or the relevant vendor tree. If it is intended only for Buildroot, the 'Upstream' status must accurately reflect why it cannot be upstreamed, or the contributor should instead provide the appropriate status (e.g., 'Pending' or 'Submitted') if upstreaming is possible.
commit_message:
This patch enables the hardware random number generator in the device tree.
Without it, the entropy level in the Linux kernel increases very slowly.
To check the entropy level: cat /proc/sys/kernel/random/entropy_avail

technical_issue: Adding a global configuration option to work around build-system pollution caused by environment variables leaking into the U-Boot build process is considered poor architectural practice. The root cause is the incorrect global export of internal Buildroot variables in package/pkg-download.mk, which should be restricted to the downloader rather than leaking into external build environments.
corrective_action: Avoid introducing new configuration options to mitigate environmental leakage. Instead, address the root cause by ensuring internal Buildroot variables are scoped correctly (e.g., within the DOWNLOAD macro or restricted to package-specific contexts) rather than globally exported.
commit_message:
The commit 4bce3270d680 ("Config.in: timeout earlier when connecting to
download servers"), among other things, adds the connection timeout
parameter to the default scp command:
config BR2_SCP
       string "Secure copy (scp) command"
       default "scp -o ConnectTimeout=10"
Since the package/pkg-download.mk file exports this command using the
SCP variable:
export SCP := $(call qstrip,$(BR2_SCP))
and the U-Boot Makefile uses this variable as the path for the system
control processor (SCP) firmware blob:
cmd_binman = $(srctree)/tools/binman/binman $(if $(BINMAN_DEBUG),-D) \
		$(foreach f,$(BINMAN_TOOLPATHS),--toolpath $(f)) \
                --toolpath $(objtree)/tools \
		$(if $(BINMAN_VERBOSE),-v$(BINMAN_VERBOSE)) \
		build -u -d $(binman_dtb) -O . -m \
		--allow-missing --fake-ext-blobs \
		$(if $(BINMAN_ALLOW_MISSING),--ignore-missing) \
		-I . -I $(srctree) -I $(srctree)/board/$(BOARDDIR) \
		$(foreach f,$(of_list_dirs),-I $(f)) -a of-list=$(of_list) \
		$(foreach f,$(BINMAN_INDIRS),-I $(f)) \
		-a atf-bl31-path=${BL31} \
		-a tee-os-path=${TEE} \
		-a ti-dm-path=${TI_DM} \
		-a opensbi-path=${OPENSBI} \
		-a default-dt=$(default_dt) \
		-a scp-path=$(SCP) \
the following error occurs:
  BINMAN  .binman_stamp
usage: binman [-h] [-B BUILD_DIR] [-D] [-H] [--tooldir TOOLDIR] [--toolpath TOOLPATH] [-T THREADS] [--test-section-timeout] [-v VERBOSITY] [-V]
              {build,bintool-docs,entry-docs,ls,extract,replace,sign,test,tool} ...
binman: error: unrecognized arguments: -o ConnectTimeout=10
make[2]: *** [Makefile:1126: .binman_stamp] Error 2
The issue was already present, as the scp command was being passed
instead of the path to the SCP firmware, but it is now detected due to
the compilation error.
Adding the BR2_TARGET_UBOOT_SCP_FIRMWARE option allows passing the
correct parameter to U-Boot, even if it is not set. This also requires
removing the SCP setting from BR2_TARGET_UBOOT_CUSTOM_MAKEOPTS for
configurations that previously set this variable explicitly.
Fixes: 4bce3270d680 ("Config.in: timeout earlier when connecting to download servers")
Suggested-by: Julien Olivain <ju.o@free.fr>
Co-Developed-by: Julien Olivain <ju.o@free.fr>
Signed-off-by: Julien Olivain <ju.o@free.fr>
Signed-off-by: Dario Binacchi <dario.binacchi@amarulasolutions.com>

technical_issue: The patch modified a file included by Makefile.am, which triggered an implicit call to automake. Buildroot target packages cannot run automake during the build process because the host-automake dependency is not yet available/satisfied when building early-stage host tools like autoconf.
corrective_action: Avoid patching files managed by autotools (like Makefile.am) that force a regeneration of build system files. If a race condition in a parallel build exists in a package that cannot trigger automake, prefer setting the package to build with MAKE1 instead of patching the Makefile logic.

technical_issue: Allowing increased patch fuzz via environment variables increases the risk of patches appearing to apply successfully while actually applying incorrectly. Buildroot enforces strict patch application (no fuzz) to guarantee integrity, and version-specific patch management should be handled through explicit directory structures rather than masking version mismatches with fuzzy application.
corrective_action: Patches must be maintained in version-specific subdirectories within the package folder to ensure they apply cleanly without fuzz. Do not use fuzz as a mechanism to reuse patches across different package versions.
commit_message:
Introduce the environment variable BR_PATCH_ARGS as a means for the
caller to pass additional arguments to the patch(1) command. These
arguments can be new arguments not originally used -- or they can
override one of the default arguments.
In addition, provide a mechanism to add patch parameters on a
per-package basis using the <PACKAGENAME>_PATCH_ARGS makefile variable,
for example "PROCPS_NG_PATCH_ARGS". This allows individual packages to
specify extra arguments or override defaults while not affecting any
other part of the build environment.
Signed-off-by: Markus Mayer <mmayer@broadcom.com>
---

 2 files changed, 5 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

technical_issue: An init script is an unreliable mechanism for setting the wireless regulatory domain because it cannot account for asynchronous kernel behaviors, driver-specific EEPROM overrides, or the race conditions inherent in regulatory database loading. Kernel-level regulatory hints are better handled via event-driven mechanisms like udev, which ensure the domain is set when the wireless module/device is initialized, regardless of the boot sequence.
corrective_action: Do not provide generic SysV init scripts for functionality that is tied to kernel module probing or hardware state. Use udev rules instead to ensure the regulatory domain is applied correctly relative to device lifecycle events, and ensure the solution is configurable via a separate file in /etc/default/.

technical_issue: Buildroot favors maintainable, sane defaults over exposing configuration options for every possible internal package setting. Allowing package-specific configuration for system groups (like vtysh group) creates unnecessary complexity and fragmentation that violates Buildroot's design philosophy of providing a consistent, standardized user environment.
corrective_action: Do not add configuration options to package Config.in files for minor customizations that deviate from standard Linux security practices, such as choosing specific system groups. If a specific system integration requires custom grouping, the user should handle this via post-build scripts or overlay files.

code_pattern:
+* +FOO_MESON_INSTALL_TAGS+, can be set to +YES+ to enable meson's `--runtime` and
+  `--devel` installation modes for the target and staging folders, respectively.
+  This configuration is necessary to prevent meson from installing development
+  artifacts into the target folder.
technical_issue: The proposed documentation change claimed the variable was necessary to prevent development artifacts from appearing in the target directory, but this is technically inaccurate because Buildroot already removes headers and development files from the target directory during the target-finalize step.
corrective_action: Do not document build variables or features based on the incorrect assumption that they are required for filesystem footprint reduction, as Buildroot handles post-build cleanup of development artifacts globally.
commit_message:
Described the usage of FOO_MESON_INSTALL_TAGS=YES which decreases
the footprint of the target since only the runtime will be installed.
All the other development header files or libraries will be installed
into the staging folder.
Signed-off-by: Vincent Jardin <vjardin@free.fr>
Suggested-by: Brandon Maier <brandon.maier@gmail.com>
---
 docs/manual/adding-packages-meson.adoc | 5 +++++
 1 file changed, 5 insertions(+)

technical_issue: The proposed change introduced inconsistent behavior by selectively installing only specific file tags (runtime/devel) for Meson-based packages, which contradicts Buildroot's established practice of installing all files to the target and handling cleanup via the target-finalize phase for all other package types.
corrective_action: Do not implement build-system-specific installation filtering that diverges from Buildroot's global 'install-all-then-finalize' workflow, unless there is a compelling, cross-package architectural reason to change the existing global policy.
commit_message:
Many packages built with Buildroot and meson currently install header
files into both:
  - the target’s /usr/include, and
  - the sysroot’s /usr/include.
When FOO_MESON_INSTALL_TAGS = YES, ensure that header files are
installed exclusively in the staging (sysroot) target during the
build process.
The default is FOO_MESON_INSTALL_TAGS ?= NO in order to avoid
changing to this new behaviour for the time being.
Signed-off-by: Vincent Jardin <vjardin@free.fr>
Suggested-by: Robin Jarry <robin@jarry.cc>
Suggested-by: Brandon Maier <brandon.maier@gmail.com>

 1 file changed, 8 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)

technical_issue: Modifying package prompt strings to include warning text is discouraged as it pollutes the Kconfig menu with non-standard labels, and expressing developer frustration or policy grievances via package metadata is inappropriate.
corrective_action: Package status and external repository recommendations should be documented in the package's readme.txt rather than modifying the package prompt or injecting meta-commentary into Config.in files.
commit_message:
Because updating mender is a complicated package, and maintainers have their
hands full with other packages, existing efforts to update the package may sit
for months at a time before being addressed or responded to. As such, the
version sitting in Buildroot is out of date by two full years! Much like the
mongodb package, one option would be to remove the package entirely.
Luckily, the official mender organization on GitHub hosts a buildroot-mender
repository with an external tree that contains up-to-date mender patches.
These patches offload the effort of the official Buildroot maintainers while
allowing users to use the latest version of mender. A win-win for both users
and maintainers of Buildroot.
Change the prompt in Config.in to direct the users to read the help text,
and in the help text, direct the users to the external tree provided by
mender.
Signed-off-by: Adam Duskett <aduskett@gmail.com>
---

 1 file changed, 5 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

-	$(Q)for p in $($(PKG)_ALL_DOWNLOADS); do \
+	$(Q)for p in "$($(PKG)_ALL_DOWNLOADS)"; do \
technical_issue: The variable $(PKG)_ALL_DOWNLOADS is a space-separated list containing multiple download targets. Wrapping the variable in double quotes causes the shell to interpret the entire list as a single string, effectively breaking the iteration logic of the for-loop.
corrective_action: Never quote variables representing space-separated lists in shell for-loops when the intent is to iterate over individual elements. For handling special characters in URLs, investigate URL-encoding or escaping techniques rather than modifying shell loop structures.

-BOOTGEN_SITE = $(call github,Xilinx,bootgen,$(BOOTGEN_VERSION))
-BOOTGEN_LICENSE = Apache-2.0
-BOOTGEN_LICENSE_FILES = LICENSE
+HOST_BOOTGEN_VERSION = xilinx_v2024.1
+HOST_BOOTGEN_SITE = $(call github,Xilinx,bootgen,$(HOST_BOOTGEN_VERSION))
+HOST_BOOTGEN_LICENSE = Apache-2.0
+HOST_BOOTGEN_LICENSE_FILES = LICENSE
technical_issue: Buildroot naming convention dictates that package metadata variables (e.g., _VERSION, _SITE, _LICENSE) should not be prefixed with 'HOST_' even for host-only packages, unless the variable value must differ between the target and host variant of the package. Using 'HOST_' for purely metadata variables is redundant and inconsistent with established codebase standards.
corrective_action: For host-only packages, define standard package metadata variables without the 'HOST_' prefix (e.g., BOOTGEN_VERSION, not HOST_BOOTGEN_VERSION). Only apply the 'HOST_' prefix to variables that specifically define build-related commands or internal logic that explicitly requires host-specific context.

technical_issue: The patch introduced unverified kernel configuration changes (CONFIG_TMPFS) without adequate justification, removed build-time logic (artifact_info generation) without updating documentation or migrating user-facing expectations, and failed to explain significant architectural deviations from upstream or existing Buildroot best practices.
corrective_action: Contributors must provide comprehensive documentation for new kernel dependencies, ensure build artifacts are generated consistently with historical package behavior or explicitly document breaking changes in migrating.adoc, and provide clear technical justification for any changes that diverge from upstream defaults or standard Buildroot infrastructure patterns.
commit_message:
Starting with Mender version 4, the program is rewritten from Go to C++ using
CMake.
The current package provided by Buildroot is 3.4.0, which is quite old, having
been released on 25/9/2022. Since then, several changes have occurred,
necessitating a substantially large patch with many changes all at once.
The following is a list of changes:
MAJOR:
  - Mender now requires a bootstrap.mender artifact.
    Introduced with version 3.5.0, the purpose of the bootstrap.mender
    artifact is on start-up; Mender checks for a special bootstrap Artifact in
    path /var/lib/mender named bootstrap.mender and installs it to initialize
    the device database when Mender first runs. This change also removes the
    requirement for the /etc/mender/artifact_info file, which Mender no longer
    uses. As such, the following are necessary for the above change:
    - Remove installing the /etc/mender/artifact_info file in the Mender.mk file.
    - Remove package/mender/artifact_info
    - Remove the creation of the artifact_info file from
      board/mender/x86_64/post-build.sh.
    - Create a generate_mender_bootstrap_artifact method in
      board/mender/x86_64/post-image-efi.sh and
      support/testing/tests/package/test_mender/post-build.sh. This method
      generates a proper bootstrap.mender file according to the documentation.
      This change also necessitates creating a "${BINARIES_DIR}"/data-part
      directory, putting the bootstrap.mender file in the data-part directory,
      and modifying mkfs.ext4 to use the data-part directory for initial
      creation.
  - Mender is now split into two separate binaries: mender-authd and
    mender-updated. Both daemons build unconditionally. However, the build
    system must invoke the `install-mender-auth` command to install the
    mender-auth daemon. Because of this, Mender's defaut mode is now
    standalone, where a user runs `mender-update install
    /path/to/update.mender` to start updates.
  - New option `BR2_PACKAGE_MENDER_AUTHD`. Select to install the mender-auth
    daemon and mender-update service files, along with the mender-auth daemon.
    Not installing the mender-auth daemon saves 3M of space when compiling
    with LTO and stripping the binary.
  - New option `BR2_PACKAGE_MENDER_IDENTITY_SCRIPTS`. Replaces automatic
    installation of identity scripts. This new option results in less future
    maintenance to the package, as any new identity scripts automatically
    install when `install-identity-scripts` is run if added in new versions of
    Mender.
  - New option `BR2_PACKAGE_MENDER_INVENTORY_SCRIPTS`. Installs several
    identity scripts automatically. Resulting in less future maintnance.
  - New option `BR2_PACKAGE_MENDER_INVENTORY_GEO`. A separate inventory script
    because it requires wget as a runtime dependency and installs by calling
    `install-inventory-network-scripts.`
  - New option `BR2_PACKAGE_MENDER_OPTIONAL_MODULES`. Replaces automatic
    installation of inventory scripts, which results in less future
    maintenance, as `install-inventory-scripts` installs the inventory scripts
    automatically. However, there is a list of update modules to remove after
    installation that the system may not support. These modules are:
      - deb: Unconditionally removed.
      - docker: If the package is not selected.
      - rpm: If the package is not selected.
  - New option `BR2_PACKAGE_MENDER_HOST_MODULES_GEN`. Installs scripts to
    HOST_DIR. These scripts intend to help generate mender artifacts commonly
    used outside of the traditional full rootfs. As they are shell scripts, there
    is no need for a host package, and a call to `install-modules-gen` with a
    DESTDIR pointing towards HOST_DIR is all that is required.
MINOR:
  - Removed from MENDER_INSTALL_CONFIG_FILES:
    - /etc/mender/scripts/version: No longer used.
    - /etc/mender/artifact_info: No longer used
    - /etc/mender/device_type: Expected to be in /var/lib/mender
    - unconditionally installing everything in $(@D)/support/modules is
      replaced with configure options sent to CMake.
    - package/mender/mender.conf:
      - Add "DeviceTypeFile": "/etc/mender/device_type"
  - Mender uses several submodules, and the tags from GitHub do not include the
    submodules in the tarball. As such, the human-readable version is above the
    commit hash as a comment in the mender.mk file, and
    `MENDER_GIT_SUBMODULES = YES` is set.

technical_issue: The rejection was due to the lack of a consistent policy for device management and module loading across defconfigs. Introducing eudev to solve dynamic module loading often forces secondary changes (like adding net.ifnames=0 to the kernel command line to suppress predictable interface names), creating fragmented and ad-hoc board configurations instead of a standardized approach.
corrective_action: Prefer mdev over eudev for dynamic module loading to maintain consistency, as mdev supports automatic module loading without the overhead of predictable network interface names, thereby avoiding unnecessary modifications to kernel command lines.

The arm64 defconfig enables both the R8169 Ethernet driver and the
PHY_ROCKCHIP_NANENG_COMBO_PHY PHY driver as modules.
Enable udev so that these modules will get automatically loaded by udev
after the rootfs is mounted from the SD card.
This way, we do not need to build these modules as built-in, and we also
get the benefit that any device plugged in to the PCIe slot will get
automatically loaded.

technical_issue: The change attempted to bypass the enforcement of hash checking when BR2_DOWNLOAD_FORCE_CHECK_HASHES is enabled. This feature is a security measure designed to ensure all downloaded files are validated against provided hashes, specifically to mitigate CVEs where unstable or unverified content could be fetched.
corrective_action: Maintain strict adherence to hash validation requirements. When BR2_DOWNLOAD_FORCE_CHECK_HASHES is enabled, all packages must undergo hash verification. If a specific package requires exceptions, ensure it does not compromise the global security policy; instead of disabling checks, use stable, versioned, or local sources for files that are inherently unstable or subject to external changes.
commit_message:
The hash check failure highlighted by test [1] reveals that
BR_NO_CHECK_HASH_FOR in package/pkg-download.mk is not correctly set
when the BR2_DOWNLOAD_FORCE_CHECK_HASHES option is enabled. The test
output shows that an attempt is made to perform a hash check on the
enterprise-numbers.txt file even though the ipmitool package has
excluded it from such verification:
...
IPMITOOL_EXTRA_DOWNLOADS += $(IPMITOOL_PEN_REG_URI)
BR_NO_CHECK_HASH_FOR += $(notdir $(IPMITOOL_PEN_REG_URI))
...
The setting of BR_NO_CHECK_HASH_FOR used by support/download/dl-wrapper
cannot be overridden by BR2_DOWNLOAD_FORCE_CHECK_HASHES because it
represents an exception that is required even when forcing hash checks
on all packages. Applying this patch shows that in the specific test,
the hash check is performed for ipmitool-1_8_19.tar.gz but not for
enterprise-numbers.txt
[1] http://autobuild.buildroot.org/results/5ae5ee948d99679cd50d1115a7d46f4368347b4f
Signed-off-by: Dario Binacchi <dario.binacchi@amarulasolutions.com>

technical_issue: The introduction of a choice...endchoice configuration prevents other packages from programmatically selecting specific dependencies of the spdlog package. In Buildroot, packages cannot 'select' a sub-option within a choice block, which breaks dependency chains for downstream packages requiring specific internal versions or modes of a library.
corrective_action: Avoid introducing choice blocks for build-time dependencies if downstream packages need to enforce specific configurations or variants. If multiple variants are required, consider separate options or simple boolean configurations that can be selected by other packages, or ensure the default behavior is robust enough that complex configuration is unnecessary.
commit_message:
Add an option menu to select the spdlog formatter: external fmt library,
external using headers-only, C++20 std:format, or the bundled (version
matched) fmt library, which each release of spdlog is tested against.
The formatters are mutually exclusive (see the CMakeLists.txt[1] file),
selecting the 'internal' option disables all the other options in the
.mk file, thus "enabling" the bundled formatter.
For backwards compatibility, the Buildroot default remains using the
external fmt library.
[1]: https://github.com/gabime/spdlog/blob/5ebfc92/CMakeLists.txt#L95-L105
Signed-off-by: Joachim Wiberg <troglobit@gmail.com>

technical_issue: The proposal added complexity by introducing multiple hook points (pre/post) for an already existing target-finalize step without providing a compelling or necessary technical justification for why the existing single-point TARGET_FINALIZE_HOOKS was insufficient.
corrective_action: Do not introduce new build system infrastructure, such as additional hook layers, unless there is a concrete, non-trivial use case that cannot be satisfied by existing mechanisms. Proposals must demonstrate necessity over theoretical convenience.
commit_message:
Provides the ability for packages to register hooks in the finalization
process before and after Buildroot performs its own final target
changes. The pre-hook (`LIBFOO_TARGET_PRE_FINALIZE_HOOKS`) operates in
the same manner as `LIBFOO_TARGET_FINALIZE_HOOKS` did before. The newly
added post-hook (`LIBFOO_TARGET_POST_FINALIZE_HOOKS`) allows packages
to perform target modifications after Buildroot applies changes such as
rootfs overlay updates.
Signed-off-by: James Knight <james.d.knight@live.com>
---
The following is a proposal for adding support for post-finalize hooks
into the Buildroot framework. Been playing with using external package
definitions to perform some post-build tweaks to some board
configurations. While post-build scripts are a great way to perform any
late-stage target manipulation, it would be nice to be able to define
packages and register them into the menu system to make it easier to
re-use shared tweaks across multiple board configuration (versus trying
to add/stack post-build script paths into each configuration).
While `LIBFOO_TARGET_FINALIZE_HOOKS` worked for most cases, this occurs
before Buildroot performs additional finalization changes (e.g. merged
usr/). It would be nice to allow packages to perform their own
finalization changes after Buildroot does the bulk of it's own
finalization, but before post-build scripts are invoked.
If this change is considered, it appears the `luarocks.mk` package would
also need to be updated (in addition to manual changes).

technical_issue: The Buildroot infrastructure treats BR2_EXTERNAL paths as a space-separated list within the Makefile context. Attempting to support paths with spaces via quoting in the generation script is insufficient, as the build system will fail to correctly parse or process paths containing spaces once they are consumed by Make.

technical_issue: The implementation was rejected due to unnecessary complexity, reliance on globbing wildcards, and suboptimal coding style (use of negative logic ifneq). Furthermore, the maintainers identified that the feature was redundant because users can already manage directory structures in custom DTS paths by adjusting internal #include directives rather than forcing structural changes in the Buildroot build logic.
corrective_action: When adding new features to build logic, prefer positive conditional logic (ifeq), avoid complex wildcard handling within makefiles, and ensure that the requested functionality cannot be achieved through existing user-space configuration (e.g., adjusting #include paths in source files) before modifying build infrastructure.
commit_message:
Linux organizes devicetree files under vendor subdirectories for arm and
arm64 architectures, such as arch/<arch>/boot/dts/<vendor>/. The
BR2_LINUX_KERNEL_CUSTOM_DTS_PATH was only copying devicetree files to
arch/<arch>/boot/dts/, which is incompatible with the vendor
subdirectory structure.
This patch adds support for using a directory in
BR2_LINUX_KERNEL_CUSTOM_DTS_PATH that matches the vendor subdirectory name.
Signed-off-by: Kory Maincent <kory.maincent@bootlin.com>

technical_issue: The proposed implementation adds unnecessary complexity to the core Buildroot package infrastructure for a feature that can be easily achieved by users via filesystem overlays or post-build scripts.
corrective_action: Avoid adding invasive features to core packages that can be implemented by the user; prefer keeping the codebase simple and leverage existing mechanisms like root filesystem overlays or post-build scripts for custom system configurations.
commit_message:
One of the major drawbacks of classic init/rc systems is that dead
daemons remain dead: Once started by the rc scripts, they are left to
look after themselves.
A simple approach to keep them running reliably is to supervise them:
In a tree with PID 1 at its root, every process knows which children
ought to run under it, and it respawns them accordingly. This is the
supervision tree.
Classic init already provides rudimentary supervision in the form of
inittab. This patch uses inittab to establish a more flexible
supervision tree based on runit's rvsvdir or s6's s6-svscan.
BR2_TARGET_SUPERVISION_TREE could be extended to other init systems
(systemd and OpenRC) as well, but I haven't done it because both of
them have their own supervision features.
Signed-off-by: J. Neuschäfer <j.neuschaefer@gmx.net>

technical_issue: Adding a 'depends on' creates a mandatory dependency for a package that can technically function as a standalone artifact without that dependency. Similar to tools like genimage, host-side utilities used in post-build scripts should be enabled by the end-user in the defconfig, not forced as a build dependency in the package Config.in, to allow for alternative workflows or toolchains.
corrective_action: Do not force host-side utility dependencies in package Config.in files if the package itself does not require them for its own compilation or installation process. If the utility is only required for final image generation (e.g., post-build scripts), expect the user to enable the required host package explicitly in their defconfig.

technical_issue: The proposal to remove the package was rejected because the maintainers confirmed the tool is still valued, functional, and actively maintained by the community, even if it requires significant effort to keep current. Buildroot does not use telemetry to justify removing packages that lack usage metrics, and existence in the tree is preferred over removal if the project is considered useful.
corrective_action: Do not submit patches to remove functional packages based on personal assessment of usage or perceived maintenance burden; instead, focus efforts on resolving build system issues, bumping versions, or coordinating with upstream developers to improve the package's integration.
commit_message:

technical_issue: The maintainers rejected forcing the disablement of stack protection based on global configuration because Buildroot hardening settings represent a minimum baseline; packages are permitted and encouraged to implement stronger hardening measures (like SSP) than the global default, even when the user has opted for 'none' globally.
corrective_action: Do not override or disable hardening features (such as SSP, relro, or fortify) at the package level simply because they are disabled in the global configuration, unless there is a critical technical failure (e.g., toolchain incompatibility) preventing the build.
commit_message:
The openssh package enables the use of compiler's stack protection by
default, but allows this feature to be disabled using the
"--without-stackprotect" configuration option.
The patch disables the compilation of openssh with the compiler's stack
protector if the Buildroot option BR2_SSP_NONE is selected, thus ensuring
that the package is built consistently with the Buildroot configuration.
All this also affects the size of the binaries created by the compilation.
Signed-off-by: Dario Binacchi <dario.binacchi@amarulasolutions.com>
---
 package/openssh/openssh.mk | 1 +
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)

technical_issue: Buildroot defines the selected stack-protection (SSP) level as a minimum requirement; packages should not explicitly disable security features just because the global Buildroot configuration specifies no SSP.
corrective_action: Do not explicitly pass --disable-ssp or similar flags based on BR2_SSP_NONE; allow the build system to utilize higher levels of stack protection if supported by the toolchain, unless an explicit build or runtime failure necessitates a change.
commit_message:
The sudo package enables the use of compiler's stack protection by
default, but allows this feature to be disabled using the "--disable-ssp"
configuration option.
The patch disables the compilation of sudo with the compiler's stack
protector if the Buildroot option BR2_SSP_NONE is selected, thus
ensuring that the package is built consistently with the Buildroot
configuration. All this also affects the size of the binaries created by
the compilation.
Signed-off-by: Dario Binacchi <dario.binacchi@amarulasolutions.com>

code_pattern: Add fragment for TI tree integration
technical_issue: The use of a configuration fragment for integrating vendor-specific trees was rejected because it causes maintenance overhead, creates potential conflicts with upstream baselines, and is not a standard pattern. Buildroot prefers independent, full-fledged defconfigs over fragments for hardware platforms to ensure clarity and version consistency.
corrective_action: Do not use configuration fragments for platform-specific tree integration. If a vendor-specific BSP is required alongside mainline support, provide a separate, complete defconfig with a clear naming convention (e.g., [platform]_vendor_defconfig) and use BR2_GLOBAL_PATCH_DIR to handle specific version hashes rather than modifying global configuration files.
commit_message:
This commit introduces a new fragment config aimed at integration of TI trees
into the existing configuration for am62x-sk. The fragment config is designed 
to be merged with the current ti_am62x_sk_defconfig, enabling transition from 
mainline to TI versions for U-boot and kernel.
The fragment config will be merged with existing ti_my_am62x_sk_defconfig
using merge-config.sh script present in support/kconfig using following
command.
./support/kconfig/merge-config.sh configs/ti_am62x_sk_defconfig configs/ti_am62x_sk.config
It effectively changes uboot and kernel source from mainline version to TI 
version hosted here
https://git.ti.com/cgit/ti-linux-kernel/ti-linux-kernel/?h=ti-linux-6.1.y
https://git.ti.com/cgit/ti-u-boot/ti-u-boot/?h=ti-u-boot-2023.04
Furthermore, as ti-uboot 2023.04 is used, kernel Image and dtb is expected 
in rootfs/boot/ and rootfs/boot/dtb/ti respectively. So add a new post-build
script to copy these files to desired location. 
Signed-off-by: Paresh Bhagat <p-bhagat@ti.com>

technical_issue: Disabling parallel builds globally using MAKE1 is a workaround that masks underlying race conditions in the upstream Makefile's parallel execution logic. Furthermore, the issue was identified as a dependency conflict between the package's .NOTPARALLEL usage and newer GNU Make versions, suggesting that the fix should be applied upstream rather than forcing serial builds in Buildroot.
corrective_action: Do not use MAKE1 to resolve build failures unless the issue is proven to be unfixable in the upstream package. Instead, investigate the underlying race condition (often related to missing dependencies in Makefile rules) and submit a patch to the upstream project to fix the build system, or apply a fix within the package's build process that addresses the specific race condition without disabling parallelism.
commit_message:
fwts randomly fail to build in parallel with error:

    mv: cannot stat 'prparser.tab.c': No such file or directory
This is a known upstream issue. See:
https://github.com/fwts/fwts/issues/7
This commit workaround this issue by disabling parallel builds.

technical_issue: The patch was rejected because it failed to demonstrate a verified build failure within the Buildroot autobuilder infrastructure and lacked an 'Upstream:' tag, which is a mandatory requirement for all patches in Buildroot.
corrective_action: All patches must be submitted to the project's upstream maintainers first, and the resulting patch file must contain an 'Upstream:' tag referencing the submission URL. Additionally, proposed patches must be verified against Buildroot's autobuilder environment to ensure they address a reproducible issue.
commit_message:
Older C standards allowed empty parentheses in function declarations,
but GCC 15 enforces C23 semantics where extern int getopt() is
equivalent to extern int getopt(void), which conflicts with the
typed prototype provided by system headers (e.g. musl's unistd.h).
Fix by providing the full prototype in the non-GNU fallback branch,
matching the standard getopt() signature.
Signed-off-by: Shubham Chakraborty <chakrabortyshubham66@gmail.com>
---

 - Identified GCC 15 (not musl) as root cause

corrective_action: Post-image scripts should avoid complex parsing of build configuration via external make calls. When handling device trees, ensure the implementation explicitly distinguishes between primary DTBs and overlays and addresses potential ShellCheck warnings related to variable expansion and command execution.
commit_message:
Users will ultimately need the BR2_LINUX_KERNEL_CUSTOM_DTS_DIR config to
import custom dts files for custom versal2 hardware. Improve the post-image.sh
to support either the default device tree which comes from u-boot or a
custom device tree for custom hardware.
To keep the example post-image script as simple as possible, it assumes that
the first DTB in the LINUX_DTBS list is the one for creating the system.dtb
filename that u-boot will use for booting. Users are free to implement their
own post-image scripts for use cases with multiple DTBs.

commit_message:
For release notes, see:
https://go.dev/doc/devel/release#go1.26.0
Building Go 1.26 and later requires Go 1.24.6 or later for bootstrap.
To support this we use Go version 1.25.8 as the version for
go-bootstrap-stage5 and have the build for Go 1.26.1 depend on
go-bootstrap-stage5.
Go version 1.25.8 is the latest Go version we can build using
go-bootstrap-stage4.
The package build for go-bootstrap-stage5 is effectively identical to
go-bootstrap-stage4 with only the Go version and stage number changed.
Go 1.28 is expected to require a minor release of Go 1.26 for bootstrap.
Signed-off-by: Christian Stewart <christian@aperture.us>

technical_issue: Using a raw git commit hash instead of a version tag obscures the package version and can cause build failures when GitHub encounteres naming collisions between tags and branches. The use of $(call github,...) without explicit reference instructions can resolve to ambiguous git objects.
corrective_action: When referencing tagged releases on GitHub that encounter ambiguity, use the explicit reference path 'refs/tags/v$(VERSION)' as the third argument to the github helper instead of substituting the version with a git commit hash.

code_pattern:
ifeq ($(BR2_POWERPC_SOFT_FLOAT),y)
# Explicitly disable PowerPC accelerations
ZLIB_NG_CONF_OPTS += -DWITH_POWER8=OFF -DWITH_POWER9=OFF
else
ifeq ($(BR2_powerpc_power8),y)
ZLIB_NG_CONF_OPTS += -DWITH_POWER8=ON
technical_issue: The proposed patch used a global if-else block that separates the architecture-specific acceleration flags from the soft-float check, leading to redundant logic and potential maintainability issues if more architecture flags are added later.
corrective_action: Ensure that soft-float constraints are integrated directly into the specific hardware feature detection logic (e.g., using a colon-separated if-condition 'ifeq ($(BR2_powerpc_power8):$(BR2_POWERPC_SOFT_FLOAT),y:)') to keep feature toggles self-contained and readable.

technical_issue: Enabling the TCG interpreter unconditionally when a native TCG backend is available results in configure-time warnings and potential conflicts, as the interpreter is intended only for architectures lacking native TCG support.
corrective_action: Conditionally enable --enable-tcg-interpreter only for target architectures that are not natively supported by QEMU's TCG backend (e.g., non-64-bit architectures or specific unsupported variants), rather than applying the flag globally.
commit_message:
Qemu has two flags related to enabling the Tiny Code Generator (TCG)
feature. Using:
  --enable-tcg
Simply enables the TCG backend for JIT code generation. However, some
architectures aren't supported by the code generator, and the build
will fail during configuration, such as in this bootlin-mipsel32r6-glibc
build:
../meson.build:923:6: ERROR: Problem encountered: Unsupported CPU , try --enable-tcg-interpreter
Enabling the TCG interpreter [0] allows for a software interpreter to
execute TCG code in cases where native code generation is unsupported,
which fixes the build.
[0] https://wiki.qemu.org/Features/TCI
Signed-off-by: Joseph Kogut <joseph.kogut@gmail.com>
---

 1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

technical_issue: The proposed fix addresses a build failure specific to GCC 15 and musl libc by globally forcing an older C standard, but the root cause of the incompatibility (likely related to code standards compliance in the package source) is not understood. Applying a workaround without investigating if an upstream-compatible patch exists or why the failure is specific to musl is considered premature.
corrective_action: Investigate the root cause of the build failure to determine if it is a standards-compliance issue solvable by a proper patch submitted to upstream. If a toolchain-specific workaround is deemed necessary as a last resort, ensure the implementation is justified by a technical analysis of why it fails on specific C libraries and attempt to utilize cleaner patching mechanisms rather than forcing CFLAGS globally.

technical_issue: The help text for legacy configuration options violated Buildroot coding standards regarding indentation and formatting, specifically failing the 'make check-package' validation for help text style.
corrective_action: Help text in Config.in files must follow strict formatting rules: exactly one tab for the first line and one tab plus two spaces for subsequent lines, and must comply with column width limits. Contributors should always run 'make check-package' before submitting patches to verify these stylistic requirements.
commit_message:
Building php-zmq is broken with php 8.5.
Although some pull requests exist upstream to fix php 8.5 support:
https://github.com/zeromq/php-zmq/pulls
they do not fix other bugs like broken build with gcc 15.x which was the
cause for the its removal by Debian:
https://tracker.debian.org/news/1668817/php-zmq-removed-from-testing/
https://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=1097580
According to various sources the package is unmaintained:
https://pecl.php.net/package/zmq
https://lists.zeromq.org/pipermail/zeromq-dev/2019-August/033109.html
https://github.com/zeromq/php-zmq/pull/240#issuecomment-3684566367
so we drop the package from buildroot.

commit_message:
Support STM32h747i-disco board. It includes an STM32H747XI SoC with the
following resources:
 - 2 Mbytes Flash
 - 1 Mbyte SRAM
 - LCD-TFT controller
 - MIPI-DSI interface
 - FD-CAN

technical_issue: When a package depends on another package that has complex dependency chains (e.g., depends on certain toolchain features), merely using 'depends on' is insufficient. It is mandatory to use 'select' to express the dependency and replicate the entire chain of 'depends on' conditions from the parent package to ensure the configuration remains consistent.
corrective_action: For dependent packages in Buildroot, use 'select' to trigger the necessary dependency and ensure the 'depends on' logic of the parent package is fully replicated in the child package's Config.in to prevent invalid build configurations.
commit_message:
Python CFFI bindings for the sysrepo YANG datastore. This package
provides the 'sysrepo' Python module which talks to the C sysrepo
library.
Also fix sysrepo build on riscv32 where glibc doesn't define
SYS_futex (only SYS_futex_time64). Use pthread-based condition
variables instead of futex on this architecture.

corrective_action: Ensure that package version bumps are accompanied by a thorough verification of all optional and mandatory dependencies. technical_issue: When building host packages that depend on LLVM, the build system may inadvertently detect and use the host machine's system-installed llvm-config instead of the version built and provided by Buildroot, leading to version mismatch errors during compilation.
corrective_action: Always explicitly define the LLVM_CONFIG environment variable in the host package configuration (HOST_CONF_ENV) to point to the Buildroot-provided llvm-config within $(HOST_DIR)/bin/ to ensure hermetic builds and prevent accidental leakage of host-provided toolchain dependencies.
commit_message:
From: Thomas Devoogdt <thomas.devoogdt@barco.com>
Meson's custom LLVM parser uses llvm-config with a default search path of
/usr/bin, causing it to detect the host system's llvm-config (version 18.1.3)
instead of the buildroot-compiled one. This forces all LLVM-related packages to
match version 18.1.3, but since the host system lacks llvmspirvlib, the build
fails. This patch forces Meson to use the buildroot-compiled llvm-config.
Note that LLVM_CONFIG is a CMake option, not a Meson one. This is because
Meson has custom dependency resolution logic for LLVM (see
https://mesonbuild.com/Dependencies.html#llvm). The EXTRA_BINARIES mechanism
cannot be used here, as it only applies to cross-compilation scenarios, which
does not apply to host-mesa3d builds.
Fixes:
  llvm-config found: YES (/usr/bin/llvm-config-18) 18.1.3
  Run-time dependency LLVM (modules: bitwriter, core, coverage, engine, executionengine, instcombine, irreader, libdriver, linker, lto, mcdisassembler, mcjit, native, option, scalaropts, target, transformutils, all-targets, coroutines, frontenddriver, frontendhlsl, lto, windowsdriver) found: YES 18.1.3
  Dependency LLVMSPIRVLib found: NO. Found 15.0.0.0 but need: '>= 18.1' ; matched: '>= 15.0.0.0', '< 18.2'
  Run-time dependency llvmspirvlib found: NO (tried cmake)
  output/build/host-mesa3d-25.0.6/meson.build:1882:21: ERROR: Dependency lookup for LLVMSPIRVLib with method 'pkgconfig' failed: Invalid version, need 'LLVMSPIRVLib' ['>= 18.1'] found '15.0.0.0'.
  A full log can be found at /home/thomas/buildroot/output/build/host-mesa3d-25.0.6/buildroot-build/meson-logs/meson-log.txt
  make[1]: *** [package/pkg-generic.mk:263: /home/thomas/buildroot/output/build/host-mesa3d-25.0.6/.stamp_configured] Error 1
  make: *** [Makefile:83: _all] Error 2
Signed-off-by: Thomas Devoogdt <thomas.devoogdt@barco.com>
---

    Also updated the git message wrt LLVM_CONFIG option.

corrective_action: When defining the root dependency in an SBOM generator script, use the user-provided project identifier (e.g., args.project_name) instead of a hardcoded string like 'buildroot' to ensure correct graph visualization in analysis tools.

technical_issue: Filtering packages by 'install_target' does not automatically prune dependencies that are host-only or absent from the component dictionary, leading to incomplete or inconsistent SBOM dependency graphs.
corrective_action: When filtering packages in dependency-parsing utilities, you must ensure the recursive dependency resolution logic validates that each dependency exists within the primary dictionary before adding it to the result set, preventing the inclusion of host-only packages or ghost references.

technical_issue: The script output is intended to be piped directly into other tools or files; injecting human-readable metadata or verbose status messages into stdout corrupts the generated data stream.
corrective_action: Auxiliary diagnostic or logging information must be directed to stderr, or alternatively, controlled via an explicit command-line argument that directs such output to a specific file or logging stream, rather than polluting the primary stdout.

code_pattern: if not comp["virtual"] else {}), and if virtual or show_info_dict.get(dep, {}).get("virtual") is False:
technical_issue: The patch attempted to globally filter out virtual packages and non-source-tracked components from the CycloneDX SBOM. This logic breaks the dependency graph and removes packages that, while lacking external download sources, are still critical components of the system's runtime architecture (e.g., init systems like tinyinit, or providing packages for virtual dependencies).
corrective_action: Maintain the integrity of the dependency graph by including virtual packages in the SBOM generation to preserve correct parent-child relationships. If filtering is necessary for specific reporting, use a flag (e.g., --virtual) to make the exclusion optional rather than hardcoding the removal in the dependency resolution logic.
commit_message:

technical_issue: Installing package-specific headers into a top-level directory named 'include' within site-packages creates an importable 'include' module. This violates Python package namespace semantics and causes potential import conflicts with existing third-party packages named 'include'.
corrective_action: Avoid polluting the site-packages namespace with top-level directories that coincide with valid Python module names. When installing headers or support files, ensure they are nested within the package's own namespace or use a different installation strategy (such as patching metadata files like _version.py) to avoid creating conflicting top-level modules.

technical_issue: The submission failed to adhere to Buildroot coding style standards, specifically regarding help text indentation, and exhibited git email formatting issues (line wrapping) that corrupted the hash file. Additionally, the maintainer raised concerns regarding the project's upstream maturity and necessity.
corrective_action: Contributors must use 'git send-email' to prevent line wrapping, verify the package using './utils/test-pkg' for toolchain compatibility, run 'make check-package' to validate coding style, and ensure help text in Config.in follows the strict one-tab plus two-spaces indentation rule.

technical_issue: Disabling BusyBox applets via automatic build-system fixups is discouraged because BusyBox applets provide redundancy (e.g., for initramfs usage) and the standard installation order correctly handles precedence where full-blown packages override BusyBox applets. Additionally, modifying BusyBox configurations via automatic fixups is considered unnecessary complexity that shifts the burden of security and configuration management from the user to the build system, which is contrary to Buildroot's design philosophy of using user-provided configurations for such needs.
corrective_action: Do not attempt to disable BusyBox applets automatically when a full-blown package is installed. If a user needs to harden their system by reducing the BusyBox attack surface, they should be instructed to provide a custom BusyBox configuration file rather than implementing programmatic fixups within package makefiles.

technical_issue: Attempting to force-disable BusyBox applets via Kconfig fixups is considered an improper way to handle package conflicts in Buildroot; instead, package selection conflicts should be managed by explicit dependencies or 'depends on' logic in Config.in, and existing unnecessary BUSYBOX_DEPENDENCIES must be cleaned up rather than introducing new config hacks.
corrective_action: Do not use KCONFIG_DISABLE_OPT to resolve applet conflicts. Instead, use 'depends on' in the package Config.in to prevent simultaneous selection of conflicting packages and remove any redundant entries from BUSYBOX_DEPENDENCIES in busybox.mk.

technical_issue: The configuration option BR2_LINUX_KERNEL_CUSTOM_DTS_PATH is defined as accepting a specific list of file paths, not a pattern-based expansion. Enabling wildcard expansion complicates build reproducibility and deviates from the intended configuration mechanism, which requires explicit file declarations.
corrective_action: Do not attempt to implement wildcard support for configuration variables intended for explicit file paths; users must explicitly list all individual .dts files required for the build.
commit_message:
There are a few config/ entries where wildcards are already present:
     $ grep -rle 'BR2_LINUX_KERNEL_CUSTOM_DTS_PATH=.*\*' configs/
     configs/octavo_osd32mp1_brk_defconfig
     configs/octavo_osd32mp1_red_defconfig
...however, while the copy-in step of device tree compilation works
correctly, these wildcards are not converted to DTC build targets in the
kernel build. As a result, this configuration line alone is not enough
to generate DTCs. It should be.
This patch adds a $(wildcard) expansion step into generation of DTB
compilation targets (the LINUX_DTB_NAME variable). As a result,
wildcards behave correctly.
Signed-off-by: Graeme Smecher <gsmecher@threespeedlogic.com>
Reviewed-by: Alexander Sverdlin <alexander.sverdlin@gmail.com>

 linux/linux.mk | 2 +-

technical_issue: The proposed CPE ID mapping is outdated, potentially inaccurate for current and future versions, and risks creating false security reporting because upstream naming conventions are inconsistent and the NVD record for the package is not actively maintained for recent releases.
corrective_action: Do not add CPE ID information for packages where the NVD database entry is stale, inaccurate, or fails to reflect current upstream naming conventions, as this leads to unreliable security vulnerability tracking.

technical_issue: The maintainer rejected the patch because an application using ALSA can be configured to run alongside PulseAudio through runtime wrappers (e.g., padsp), user-defined configurations, or by ensuring the two are not accessing the hardware concurrently. Untested compatibility does not justify a build-time hard exclusion in Buildroot.
corrective_action: Do not introduce build-time dependencies or mutual exclusions based on unconfirmed runtime incompatibilities unless the software is strictly and definitively incapable of coexisting with the package in question.
commit_message:
According to the README.md file:
```
[The] plugin was tested to work with ALSA and `pulseaudio` might prevent the
      plugin from playing audio correctly:
    - Hence please make sure you delete `pulseaudio` package from your system.
```
And from plugins/audioplayers/README.md:
```
...
- Make sure `pulseaudio` is deleted
Please note that plugin was not tested with `pulseaudio` and it is up to you
to make gstreamer work via it. As `pulseaudio` takes full control over audio
devices, `ALSA` will no longer function correctly with `pulseaudio` installed
```
As such, it is best to ensure the pulseaudio package is not selected to
ensure the audio player plugin works out of the box.
Signed-off-by: Adam Duskett <adam.duskett@amarulasolutions.com>

technical_issue: The patch added a user-configurable string option for a cache location, which is unnecessary and potentially harmful. Hard-coding cache locations inside the $(BR2_DL_DIR) directory is the standard practice in Buildroot to ensure persistent caching across builds and consistent management, rather than exposing configuration options for internal build artifacts.
corrective_action: Do not introduce Kconfig options for internal build cache directories. Instead, use a fixed path derived from $(BR2_DL_DIR) to ensure consistency with other packages (e.g., cargo-home) and facilitate persistent storage that survives clean/distclean cycles.
commit_message:
When running the command "flutter pub get," the plugins are stored in the
pub-cache directory along with their sha256sum hashes. The default location of
the pub-cache directory is current $(HOST_DIR)/share/flutter/sdk/.pub-cache,
which is not an acceptable choice by default because every plugin is
re-downloaded during every build of a flutter application either during a new
build or when building with the per-package-directory option enabled.
Furthermore, keeping the pub-cache in its current location prevents users from
committing the pub-cache directory to git for faster rebuilds of a
Buildroot-based system, as a user cannot store the pub-cache for later use.
To fix the above issue, add a new option to flutter-sdk-bin/Config.in.host,
BR2_PACKAGE_HOST_FLUTTER_SDK_BIN_PUB_CACHE_LOCATION and set the default to
$(DL_DIR)/br-flutter-pub-cache. While hard-coding the location is generally
preferable, a developer may already have a working pub-cache at a different
location. Setting this option to a string provides no downsides, and the
default location is now set to a sane default, providing an optimal solution
to the problem above.
Signed-off-by: Adam Duskett <adam.duskett@amarulasolutions.com>

Rather than ignoring the CVE in Buildroot, the correct approach is to rely on existing version-based tracking or update the upstream NVD database, as the CVE is not inherently 'disputed' but rather 'already resolved' in upstream releases.
corrective_action: Do not add disputed or resolved CVEs to the IGNORE_CVES list if the issue is already mitigated by an upstream version bump. Instead, verify if the CVE status can be corrected in the upstream NVD/security database or if the Buildroot package version already includes the fix.

technical_issue: The removal of the artifact_info file generation breaks the Mender integration process, as the build process for a Mender-enabled system must explicitly define the artifact name for the image to be valid and deployable by the Mender client.
corrective_action: Do not remove essential configuration files required for runtime services; if an option is deprecated or changed, ensure the alternative mechanism for generating the required files (like artifact_info) is correctly implemented and tested to prevent CI regression.
commit_message:
The artifact_info file is no longer supported. Instead, mender now expects a
bootstrap.mender artifact created by mender-artifact in either /data/ or
/var/lib/mender. See the following for more information:
https://northerntech.atlassian.net/browse/MEN-2585
https://northerntech.atlassian.net/browse/MEN-2583
  - Remove all instances of creating or copying an artifact_info file
    from board examples and package/mender.
  - Add a generate_mender_bootstrap_artifact method to
    board/mender/x86_64/post-image-efi.sh which creates a proper
    bootstrap.mender file and places it in the data partition.
  - Add a post-build.sh script to support/testing/tests/package/test_mender
    with the same generate_mender_bootstrap_artifact method with one change:
    As the rootfs.ext4 file is not yet created; we omit the optional
    --provides "rootfs-image.checksum:${img_checksum}" argument when
    generating the bootstrap.mender file.
  - With the new bootstrap.mender requirements, one other problem needs to be
    addressed. Currently, due to the possibility of a user creating a
    read-only filesystem in mender.mk we link /var/run/mender to
    /var/lib/mender. In S42mender the following happens:
    """
    # If /var/lib/mender is a symlink to /var/run/mender, and
    #   - the filesystem is RO (i.e., we can not rm the symlink),
    #     create the directory pointed to by the symlink.
    #   - the filesystem is RW (i.e., we can rm the symlink),
    #     replace the symlink with an actual directory
    """
However, with the new requirement of a bootstrap.mender file, it is likely that
a user will generate and place the boostrap.mender file in
$(TARGET_DIR)/var/lib/mender in a post-build script, causing subsequent rebuilds
to fail. As such, add one new line: rm -rf $(TARGET_DIR)/var/lib/mender to
ensure we can re-create the symlink without rebuild errors.

In order to use all the features of KGDB it's nice to have
the possibility to source the vmlinux-gdb.py file. The
generation of this file depend on CONFIG_GDB_SCRIPTS config
option. Add it in order to use gdb of vmlinux in output directory
including tx-* linux script
gdb-multiarch --tui output/build/<linux dir>/vmlinux
add-auto-load-safe-path output/build/<linux dir>
source output/build/<linux dir>/vmlinux-gdb.py
Linux needs to be compiled with DEBUG_INFO (without restrict
DEBUG_INFO) and kernel cmdline should be modified to wait
of kgdb if needed. As example
kgdboc_earlycon=ns16550a kgdboc=/dev/ttyS2 kgdbwait nokaslr
The test was done on am62x board over serial line. In order
to have it running properly we need to compile the agent-proxy
./output/host/bin/agent-proxy 4440 4441 0 /dev/ttyUSB0 115200
gdb can now having the debugging uart available using:
target remote localhost:4441
the 4440 is used by the console and 4441 is used to debug
[Yann E. MORIN: suggest to change the gdb.mk file instead,

technical_issue: The proposed implementation required complex host-target communication (e.g., networking) to analyze minidumps on the target, which contradicts the design philosophy of Google Breakpad. Breakpad is intended for offline analysis on the host using debugging symbols generated during the build process, rather than relying on on-target tools or runtime symbol extraction.
corrective_action: Runtime tests for debugging tools must follow the intended architectural use-case: target applications should generate crash dumps which are then retrieved to the host system for analysis using host-side tools like 'minidump_stackwalk', rather than attempting to perform analysis or symbol-heavy operations on the target device.
commit_message:
Add a test that does the following:
  - Builds the example code found here:
    https://chromium.googlesource.com/breakpad/breakpad/+/master/docs/linux_starter_guide.md
  - runs the google-breakpad-example program and checks to ensure the exit
    code is 139 (SIGSEGV)
  - Runs the minidump_stackwalk tool against the google-breakpad-example
    symbols and checks for the following:
    - That the google-breakpad-examples symbols loaded properly.
    - That the crash() method in the google-breakpad-example.cpp file is
      printed.
Signed-off-by: Adam Duskett <adam.duskett@amarulasolutions.com>

commit_message:
From: Giulio Benetti <giulio.benetti+tekvox@benettiengineering.com>
If SWUpdate is built using -Os optimization it will fail to update using
Mongoose webserver randomly and always while using option
installed-directly in .swu file generation. So let's workaround the bug
by building with -O3.
Reported upstream:

technical_issue: The existing package was already providing memtest86+ functionality under the name memtest86. Renaming the package directory and config symbols is unnecessary overhead; the preferred approach for addressing naming discrepancies is to update the prompt text within the existing Config.in file rather than performing a full rename, which avoids breaking user configurations and maintainer mappings.
corrective_action: Do not perform a full package rename for cosmetic reasons if the existing package name is functional; instead, update the user-facing prompt to reflect the correct product name while maintaining existing package directory structures and Config.in symbols for backward compatibility.

technical_issue: The test relied on checking for a PID file or systemd status to verify service readiness, which is unreliable as it does not confirm if the service is actually functional or if the application is crashing in a loop. Furthermore, redundant checks for service state are discouraged when a functional verification (e.g., executing a database query) provides a more accurate validation of service health.
corrective_action: Runtime tests should validate functional success by directly interacting with the package (e.g., executing a command or query) rather than checking intermediate state artifacts like PID files or status unit reports, unless explicitly justified by the inability to distinguish failure modes.

commit_message:
From: Peter Seiderer <ps.report@gmx.net>
Systemd creates a /etc/locale.conf file with LANG="C.UTF-8". On boot, systemd
reads /etc/locale.conf and sets the LANG environment variable,
(see the locale_context_load_conf method in local-setup.c.)
When initdb.c is called, a check for the LANG environment variable is called,
and if it is set to something other than "C" initdb attempts to load the
corresponding LC_CTYPE file in /usr/lib/locale/. IE: If LANG is set to C.UTF-8,
then initdb.c attempts to load /usr/lib/locale/C.UTF-8/LC_CTYPE. However, these
files do not exist on a Buildroot system, and as such, initdb throws the
following error on startup:
```
initdb: error: invalid locale settings; check LANG and LC_* environment variables
pg_ctl: database system initialization failed
```
To fix this issue, add "Environment=LANG=C" to the package provided
postgresql.service file to force Postgresql to use the C locale.
Tested-by: Adam Duskett <adam.duskett@amarulasolutions.com>
Signed-off-by: Adam Duskett <adam.duskett@amarulasolutions.com>
---
v1 -> v2:
  - Get to the root cause of the problem and provide a better explination of
    what is happening.
  - Use Environment=LANG=C isntead of -o --locale=C
 package/postgresql/postgresql.service | 4 ++++
 1 file changed, 4 insertions(+)

technical_issue: The change was rejected because in kconfig, boolean symbols are disabled by not setting them (or setting them as 'is not set' comments) rather than explicitly assigning them to 'n'. Furthermore, the maintainers established a policy of minimizing deviations from the upstream kernel provider's default configurations.
corrective_action: Do not attempt to override upstream-defined hardware kernel defaults in board defconfigs to match personal or third-party distribution preferences. If a behavior change is required, implement it via an overlay, startup script, or documentation update in the board/readme.txt to allow users to opt-in, and ensure any kconfig modifications follow standard 'not set' syntax.
commit_message:
* The defconfigs from the RPi Kernel set `powersave` as the default
  CPU governor, which is a bad idea as that reduces performance by
  setting the CPU frequency to the minimum one.
* In case of a buildroot build user-space is not configured by default
  to change the CPU governor, so `powersave` will remain the CPU
  governor and it will slow down everything.
* I submitted a fix for those defconfigs to upstream here:
  https://github.com/raspberrypi/linux/pull/5666
  However, we shouldn't wait until that is accepted and backported
  to all the stable branches (if ever is).
* This patch unsets the selection of `powersave` as the default CPU
  governor.
* Then the CPU governor that will be selected as default after this
  patch will be the Linux's default one that is either `ondemand` or
  `schedutil` depending on the Kernel version.
Signed-off-by: Carlos Alberto Lopez Perez <clopez@igalia.com>
---

commit_message:
The Bootlin toolchain 2023.08-1 provide (by mistake [1]) libz.so in its
sysroot and break several python test in the gitlab-ci [2] since the
python package detect zlib while the zlib package is not build. So
there is no zlib library installed to the target rootfs.
The issue [1] has been fixed in Buildroot [3] but the Bootlin toolchain
needs to be rebuild with the fix to remove libz.so from the sysroot.
With Bootlin toolchain 2023.08-1, zlib is detected by pkg-config and
enable zlib support in binascii python module (-DUSE_ZLIB_CRC32) even
if the zlib module is disabled.
If binascii python module is used at runtime, it fail to load zlib:
  Failed to run: python sample_python_pyyaml_enc.py
  output was:
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "/root/sample_python_pyyaml_enc.py", line 1, in <module>
        import yaml
      File "/usr/lib/python3.11/site-packages/yaml/__init__.py", line 8, in <module>
      File "/usr/lib/python3.11/site-packages/yaml/loader.py", line 8, in <module>
      File "/usr/lib/python3.11/site-packages/yaml/constructor.py", line 14, in <module>
      File "/usr/lib/python3.11/base64.py", line 11, in <module>
    ImportError: libz.so.1: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory
This is also a reproducible build issue since python doesn't provide
the same features if zlib is buid before or after the python build.
Fix this issue by disabling zlib support along with the zlib python
module (--disable-zlib).
Fixes:
https://gitlab.com/buildroot.org/buildroot/-/jobs/5201302864 (TestPythonPy3Pyyaml)
https://gitlab.com/buildroot.org/buildroot/-/jobs/5201302828 (TestPythonPy3Mako)
(and many other)
[1] 3341ceb1e58536cf43a4c000e4bb3cdbff3f6de5 (package/gdb: zlib is mandatory, not optional)
[2] https://gitlab.com/buildroot.org/buildroot/-/jobs/5201302864
[3] 8ce33fed49aea319da556eab388211a705afe7d0 (package/gdb: gdbserver does not need zlib)
Cc; Asaf Kahlon <asafka7@gmail.com>
Cc: James Hilliard <james.hilliard1@gmail.com>
Cc: Thomas Petazzoni <thomas.petazzoni@bootlin.com>
Cc: Yegor Yefremov <yegorslists@googlemail.com>
Signed-off-by: Romain Naour <romain.naour@gmail.com>
---

  TestPythonPy3Pyyaml (without zlib)

technical_issue: The contributor incorrectly assumed that a merged /usr filesystem was a technical requirement for the refpolicy package, whereas the package functions correctly without it. The inclusion of the dependency and comment were based on a misunderstanding of how file context aliases are handled.
corrective_action: Do not introduce restrictive dependencies (such as BR2_ROOTFS_MERGED_USR) based on assumptions about upstream configuration files; verify if the package truly requires the feature or if configuration-level aliases allow it to function under different filesystem layouts.
commit_message:
Refpolicy contexts expect a merged /usr file system. In fact, there
are no references to /bin, /sbin, /lib, or /lib64 at all. However, if a user
wants to go through the trouble of creating a policy that works with a
non-merged system, they should be free to do so. As such, only select
BR2_ROOTFS_MERGED_USR if using BR2_PACKAGE_REFPOLICY_UPSTREAM_VERSION.
Signed-off-by: Adam Duskett <adam.duskett@amarulasolutions.com>

corrective_action: Ensure that Buildroot package configuration flags for filesystem layout (like merged vs split /usr) are logically consistent with the BR2_ROOTFS_MERGED_USR setting, and verify new package configurations with a runtime test.

technical_issue: The proposed udev rule constitutes a system-wide policy change rather than a feature specific to the package. Kernel module management for generic hardware interfaces is considered a responsibility of the end-user configuration (e.g., via rootfs overlay or system configuration) rather than being baked into an application package.

/home/bernd/buildroot/output/per-package/cairo/host/bin/../lib/gcc/xtensa-buildroot-linux-uclibc/12.3.0/../../../../xtensa-buildroot-linux-uclibc/bin/ld:
 NOTE: This behaviour is deprecated and will be removed in a future version of the linker
These linker warnings were introduced by binutils 2.39:
https://www.redhat.com/en/blog/linkers-warnings-about-executable-stacks-and-segments
Silence them by adding "-Wl,-z,noexecstack" to fix cairo's pthread check
Meanwhile upstream removed autoconf support on the master branch:
https://cgit.freedesktop.org/cairo/commit/?id=efe303d9db6e24d5786c15252327811338bc9b0a
so no need to notify them.
Fixes:
http://autobuild.buildroot.net/results/408/408a900ac01dbce145aff302c75f9f4e147f5672/

technical_issue: The proposed implementation creates a 'partially correct' representation of the root filesystem that lacks essential post-processing steps (such as fakeroot permission adjustments) performed later in the build pipeline. Furthermore, the maintainers prefer a systemic architecture change (installing all packages to STAGING_DIR) over adding ad-hoc configuration options that provide incomplete or misleading filesystem states.
corrective_action: Avoid introducing ad-hoc options to capture intermediate filesystem states if they do not accurately reflect the final, finalized root filesystem. If a feature request involves complex architectural shifts, contributors should pursue the broader, intended architectural solution (e.g., global staging installation) rather than introducing temporary, incomplete build-time workarounds.
commit_message:
It is reasonable to expect that deployed systems don't contain debugging
symbols. At the very least, it will bloat image size unnecessarily.
There might also be scenarios where a previously released image might
generate core dumps, or require attaching a debugger to a process
well after the filesystem image has been made.
These two goals contradict each other, and is addressed here.
Just before stripping target binaries, allow the option to tar the
target filesystem for archiving. This will create a single image -
rootfs_syms.tgz - with un-stripped target binaries, and allow post-build
debugging and core dump analysis. The final image will be unaffected.
Signed-off-by: Colin Foster <colin.foster@in-advantage.com>
---

 Makefile  |  3 +++
 2 files changed, 13 insertions(+)

technical_issue: Validating file integrity based on static line ranges is fragile, error-prone when files are modified, and fails to handle non-contiguous or dynamic content patterns. Relying on line numbers for checksums is considered a hackish solution that does not scale.
corrective_action: For extracting license files or specific segments of files for validation, use post-extract hooks in the package recipe. Implement logic (e.g., using awk, sed patterns, or extraction scripts) to dynamically generate the required file instead of relying on hardcoded line-range checksum validation.
commit_message:
The checksum checking is done on whole files only using the <pkg>.hash
checksum references.
Among the files checked, files related to licenses are checked.
Some packages do not contain any specific license files and, for
them, some source files are used. These source files contain license
information (usually comments at the beginning of the file).
Using the whole source file for checksum checking in this case can lead
to issues if a br2-external is present and applies some patches to this
source file.
Indeed, patching a package from a br2-external is allowed but in that
case the whole file checksum change and all checksum verification done
on that file fails. In particular 'make legal-info' fails.
Using only the license related part of a source file for checksum
checking solve this issue.
'make legal-info' will fail only if the license part is modify.
Introduce the possibility to have a lines range in <pkg>.hash and, if
present, compute checksum on the part defined by this lines range.
For instance, in <pkg>.hash:
sha256  xxxxxx  foo.c        <-- sha256 on the whole foo.c file
sha256  xxxxxx  foo.c  1,15  <-- sha256 on extraction from line 1 to 15
Signed-off-by: Herve Codina <herve.codina@bootlin.com>

technical_issue: Modifying logical eraseblock (LEB) size to solve capacity issues is technically incorrect because LEB size must align with specific NAND flash geometry. Increasing the size to circumvent a 'max_leb_cnt' error masks the actual problem (insufficient image capacity for the filesystem contents) and creates incompatible filesystem images for target hardware.
corrective_action: Do not modify LEB size to address storage space limitations. Instead, increase the maximum number of logical eraseblocks (max_leb_cnt) or the total partition size to accommodate the root filesystem contents, ensuring the configuration remains consistent with the target flash geometry.

technical_issue: OpenSBI is a critical bootloader component. Removing explicit version pinning (custom version) in defconfigs prevents the ability to guarantee that specific hardware/configurations remain bootable, as versions must be validated before adoption.
corrective_action: Do not remove version pinning for critical boot components (OpenSBI, U-Boot, Kernel) in defconfigs; instead, update them to a validated newer version if necessary. For QEMU-based defconfigs, keeping them on the latest version is acceptable to align with upstream QEMU bios defaults, but it must be an explicit version update rather than removing the pinning entirely.

technical_issue: Adding package-specific Kconfig options for init script startup priorities introduces unnecessary complexity and maintenance burden, as this is a generic requirement applicable to all init scripts.
corrective_action: Avoid adding package-specific options for standard system configuration tasks like init script ordering. Use a post-build script to customize or rename files in $(TARGET_DIR) to meet specific project needs.
commit_message:
In some cases, it is necessary to explicitly specify the startup
priority of the tee-supplicant script in init.d.
In most cases of system operation, the S30 priority is acceptable.
However, there are cases when the user needs to choose the startup
priority of the tee-supplicant.
For example, for encryption/decryption of the user's file system,
OPTEE is usually required, i.e. the tee-supplicant daemon needs to
be running. Usually, such a script runs on the earliest priorities
in the system, for example, S01.
By default, the priority remains at 30, but for specific scenarios,
the user can specify their own priority.
Signed-off-by: Alexey Romanov <avromanov@sberdevices.ru>

technical_issue: The patch attempted to implement 'flexible reinstalls' for /etc/fstab, which is incompatible with Buildroot's per-package directory architecture; when packages are aggregated, changes to the skeleton file are not guaranteed to propagate to downstream packages. Additionally, modifying /etc/fstab in-place for systemd is discouraged, as systemd mount units are the preferred mechanism.
corrective_action: Do not implement logic to 'patch' or 'update' existing files within a package to support partial re-builds or incremental changes, as Buildroot only guarantees correctness for full builds from scratch. For systemd, prefer using native mount units over manipulation of /etc/fstab.
