technical_issue: The proposal introduces redundant, restricted functionality that duplicates existing capabilities. Buildroot already provides flexible mechanisms (post-build scripts) that allow for arbitrary filesystem manipulation including pruning, rendering a specific 'prune' configuration feature unnecessary and overly complex.
corrective_action: Do not add new, narrow-scope features that overlap with existing general-purpose hooks (like post-build scripts). If a task can be achieved with existing infrastructure (overlays, post-build/image/fakeroot scripts), the existing mechanisms should be used rather than introducing new configuration options.
commit_message:
The following introduces the option `BR2_ROOTFS_PRUNE`, to allow users
to provide lists of directories and files that should be pruned from the
file system after a build. This is to provide a simpler means of
defining and cleaning up paths, instead of manually creating removal
logic inside a post-build script.
Users will now be able to define a file of entries to remove and
register this file as part of a board's configuration. More than one
file can be provided. Each line in a file will represent a file or
directory that should be removed. Wildcard values are supported for file
entries when attempting to remove a group of entries from a folder.
Comments are also supported in these files, allowing users to document
reasons for desiring board-specific removals. Sanity checks exists to
help prevent users from accidentally removing files outside of the
target directory.
Signed-off-by: James Knight <james.d.knight@live.com>
---
The goal of this patch is to provide an easy way for users to define
files to prune from the target file system after a build. Typically,
projects wanting to prune would have some sort of logic inside a
post-build script. Managing support to clear out select directories/
files is not always trivial, especially when wanting to do some wildcard
patterns on files to remove. Buildroot provides nice device table and
user table definition files, and having a file with entries of paths to
remove seems like a nice way to manage such an action. Projects
performing this type of pruning could have some library logic to support
a "prune" function to do this, but it would be nice if Buildroot
provides this support out-of-box (opinion).
These changes would also need to include some manual updates. Held off
this work if this feature is not something desired.
---

 support/scripts/prune | 122 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
 system/Config.in      |  10 ++++
 3 files changed, 138 insertions(+)
 create mode 100755 support/scripts/prune

technical_issue: The patch attempted to introduce platform-specific compatibility for Windows by forcing POSIX-style paths within the utility. Buildroot is designed to function strictly within a Linux/Unix environment; providing first-class support for non-POSIX file systems (which may lack case sensitivity or handle path separators differently) is rejected to maintain codebase consistency and prevent subtle bugs.
corrective_action: Do not submit patches designed to enable Buildroot to run natively on Windows. Users requiring a Windows environment must utilize a virtual machine or a compatibility layer that provides a standard Linux environment.
commit_message:
The following includes a series of tweaks to the check-package script to
provide flexibility when sanity checking files when sources are checked
out in a Windows environment. This includes:
- When populating a list of files to check, ensure POSIX paths are used;
   otherwise, the internal list of files to check will be improperly
   formed and no checks will occur.
- When interpreting an ignore list (which provides POSIX paths of files
   to ignore), resolve the absolute file path for these entries to
   ensure files can be ignored when cycling through a provided file
   list.
- Ignore execute bit checks on Windows since X_OK is only an existence
   check on Windows.
- When running ShellCheck, always ignore SC1017 [1] to avoid line
   ending warnings since Windows environments will typically utilize
   `git config core.autocrlf`.
[1]: https://www.shellcheck.net/wiki/SC1017
Signed-off-by: James Knight <james.d.knight@live.com>
---
 utils/check-package           | 9 +++++++--
 utils/checkpackagelib/tool.py | 4 +++-
 2 files changed, 10 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)

technical_issue: Buildroot follows a principle of minimalism where features are disabled by default unless they are essential. Enabling all protocol options by default is contrary to the design goal of allowing users to enable only what they need for their specific hardware.
corrective_action: Do not attempt to enable all optional features in a package by default. Keep the package configuration minimal, requiring users to manually select only the specific protocols or features needed for their particular use case.
commit_message:
When using the gpsd package, the menu options provide a way to select
each individual protocol that is desired in the final process. However,
there is no graceful way to say "select all protocols".
This commit tweaks the menu selection to have all protocols selected by
default. For builds that want to opt out of specific protocols, they can
do so by unchecking the individual protocols as desired.
Signed-off-by: James Knight <james.d.knight@live.com>
---
The reason for this change is a combination of two things. First, when
building gpsd out-of-box, typically each protocol is enabled unless the
configuration stage requests to opt out of using certain protocols.
Buildroot's configuration takes the opposite approach by having each
protocol disabled until selected. Second, in most cases (at least in my
own experience), I typically flag each protocol manually to build. It
would be nice when managing the use of gpsd to just use all protocols
by default.
However, this approach will change a board's experience if new protocols
are added into gpsd (where new protocols would be enabled by default).

technical_issue: Buildroot cannot reliably determine if specific bug fixes or backports are present in toolchain headers based solely on kernel version metadata, as stable/dot-releases and vendor-specific backports lack granular tracking; therefore, reliance on the official major release version where a feature or fix was introduced is required for reproducible builds.
corrective_action: Do not remove header version dependencies for packages that rely on specific kernel features or bug fixes unless the requirement is no longer technically necessary across all possible toolchain variations or the official upstream minimum version requirement has decreased.
commit_message:
From: Petr Vorel <petr.vorel@gmail.com>
5e1c238b72 ("package/ltp-testsuite: require 5.11 headers for musl")
added a 5.11 headers requirement for musl, which contains fix
a85cbe6159ff ("uapi: move constants from <linux/kernel.h> to <linux/const.h>").
This effectively disables LTP on all musl based toolchains from
toolchain-configs.csv:
      bootlin-armv7-musl [ 7/45]: SKIPPED
    bootlin-riscv64-musl [19/45]: SKIPPED
     bootlin-x86-64-musl [26/45]: SKIPPED
br-i386-pentium-mmx-musl [31/45]: SKIPPED
But these toolchains are based on 4.9.255 kernel headers, which have
backported fix as 834d8b96cd85 in 4.9.250 and LTP can be compiled with
them. Because there is currently no way to specify also older stable
releases with fix, it's better to remove this requirement. If people are
really using unfixed kernels we can reintroduce the workaround which was
removed in 5e1c238b72 or even ask upstream to carry it.
Signed-off-by: Petr Vorel <petr.vorel@gmail.com>
---
Hi,
I'm sorry for not checking the build properly. v1 and v2 of LTP update
were without this because I did check them properly and saw that 5.11
version requirement for musl was not needed (thanks to Thomas, who in
the past after my asking for that had found a time and updated toolchains).
Kind regards,
Petr

config BR2_PACKAGE_URANDOM_SCRIPTS
	bool "urandom-initscripts package was renamed"
	select BR2_LEGACY
	select BR2_PACKAGE_BUILDROOT_INITSCRIPTS

technical_issue: The implementation of the migration required a legacy transition that would trigger warnings for nearly all non-systemd user configurations, creating unnecessary user inconvenience, while the architectural benefit of grouping scripts into a new package was deemed insufficient to justify such an impact.
corrective_action: Avoid refactoring or moving existing packages if the architectural improvement is minor compared to the burden imposed by the resulting mandatory legacy transition and global user config warnings.
commit_message:
As a preparation to add new initscripts, rename the urandom-scripts
package to a more generic one and prepare the package to support more
than one initscript. This way we don't need one package per initscript.

technical_issue: The patch installs a default configuration file specifically for the root user, which is contrary to Buildroot policy. Package configurations should generally not be hardcoded to specific user directories (like /root) as it lacks global usefulness and violates the principle of not imposing specific configurations on system users.
corrective_action: Do not install default configuration files for the root user in target packages. If a package requires configuration to function, it should either rely on system-wide defaults or be handled by the user or rootfs overlay configuration rather than the package installation hooks.

technical_issue: The issue was caused by a non-standard installation path (/usr/include/swupdate) in Buildroot that forced the package to use local include headers in a way that deviated from upstream standards, causing include path resolution failures.
corrective_action: Do not attempt to patch source files to accommodate non-standard directory structures created by Buildroot. Instead, fix the underlying package installation path within the build recipe (e.g., swupdate.mk) to follow upstream standards and prevent namespace collisions.

because the header is located in $STAGING_DIR/usr/include/swupdate and
not $STAGING_DIR/usr/include.

technical_issue: Modifying the PATH to include the STAGING_DIR/usr/bin directory causes the build system to mistakenly attempt to execute target-compiled binaries on the host machine, leading to runtime failures during compilation.
corrective_action: Do not manipulate PATH to include STAGING_DIR. Instead, properly configure cross-compilation by utilizing <pkg>_CONFIG_SCRIPTS or passing specific cache variables/environment variables to the build system (e.g., configure scripts) to point to the correct staging path for dependencies.

technical_issue: Buildroot operates under the principle that the user is responsible for managing system dependencies like /bin/sh. Introducing a hard configuration dependency (BR2_SYSTEM_NEEDS_BIN_SH) would prevent valid configurations—such as custom skeletons, external rootfs overlays, or external packages providing their own shell—from selecting specific init systems, thereby violating the flexibility principle of Buildroot.
corrective_action: Do not introduce global build-time restrictions for mandatory tools like /bin/sh. Assume the user is managing system requirements through skeletons or external configuration, and allow the system to remain flexible to non-standard environments.
commit_message:
Some packages will require that /bin/sh exists and is a valid shell.
For example, system needs a shell, but does not care much what it is. We
could provide it with whatever the user configures as BR2_SYSTEM_BIN_SH,
but that can be empty because of BR2_SYSTEM_BIN_SH_NONE.
So, we add an option to restrict the choice and forbid BR2_SYSTEM_BIN_SH_NONE
from being a selectable option, this ensuring that there is a shell on
the system. As a side effect, it also ensures that /bin/sh exists.
For systmd, we can then just tell it to unconditionally use /bin/sh as
the default user shell, and that will also match whatever the user
selected as the system-wide default shell.
Signed-off-by: Yann E. MORIN <yann.morin.1998@free.fr>
Cc: Norbert Lange <nolange79@gmail.com>
---
Note: this is a prerequisite for the bump to systemd 252.4:

technical_issue: Upstream git explicitly moved away from using the OpenSSL SHA implementations as the default due to security and performance considerations, preferring the bundled DC_SHA1 implementation instead. Overriding this default without a compelling, documented technical justification contradicts the upstream maintainers' design decisions regarding collision attack mitigations.
corrective_action: Do not override upstream build defaults for cryptographic backends or performance knobs unless there is a proven, documented requirement to do so for the specific Buildroot target environment. Always verify the history and rationale of build-time flags in the upstream project's repository before submitting patches.
commit_message:
Git is shipped with its own implementation of SHA-1 and SHA-256.
However, when OpenSSL is available (BR2_PACKAGE_OPENSSL=y), it is
preferable to leverage the OpenSSL version of these hash algorithm
instead. --with-openssl configure flag doesn't enable that, though.
Set OPENSSL_{SHA1,SHA256} make variable when OpenSSL package is
selected.
Signed-off-by: Bagas Sanjaya <bagasdotme@gmail.com>

technical_issue: Systemd-tmpfiles are processed after local-fs.target, making them unsuitable for creating or configuring directories required by services or mount points that must exist before system initialization completes. Relying on tmpfiles.d for filesystem setup that impacts early boot stages or filesystem mounting is unreliable due to ordering limitations.
corrective_action: Do not use tmpfiles.d configurations to manage directories required for early boot, filesystem mounting, or overlay initialization. Such requirements must be handled within the specific service unit files or init scripts responsible for those operations to ensure proper dependency ordering.

commit_message:
As the build process can be long and hard to track, I wrote a little Python
script that try to make it easier for users
to see where in the process they are, what is going on, log filtering and
more.
The script is completly autonomous and doesn't impose anything on the current
build process so it should work without
tampering the maintenability of the project's Makefile.
The script has only two dependencies :
    - packaging (for version parsing while checking Buildroot's dependencies)
    - rich (for the colored output and layout)
Features:
    - test the presence of Buildroot's dependencies, with version control and
path validation (for `file`)
    - (optional) test the presence of Buildroot's optional dependencies but
without blocking the process
    - can only do the checking or skip it entirely using command line option
    - filter the output of the build process while trying to detect
fatal/error/warning/debug loglevel (may have some false positive)
    - can change the filtering level using a command line option (default on
ERROR)
    - keep track of the amount of work to do using `make external-deps` and
display the overall progress
    - in case the amount of work isn't correctly detected (I try to account for
some specials steps), the progress is fixed at the end with a message
    - keep track of all the task already done and the one currently working
    - display the current task's step message (extracted from `>>> %s` output)
    - keep track of the current task progress based on the amount of steps
done
    - display a progress in case of `wget` with extraction of filename, size,
and downloaded from the output
The script is launching `make` by itself but the user is supposed to have
already done the configuration needed!
May be added later:
    - export raw output from `make` into a log file in /tmp or elsewhere
(command line option?)
    - add other trackable step like `wget`?
    - improve loglevel detection
    - any request from the team?!
Some images can be seen on my personal server:
    - the `--help` menu: https://naheulcraft.be/nextcloud/s/aX8fW5mK8dbXwqT
    - the progress tracking:
https://naheulcraft.be/nextcloud/s/87GK7yXQzZHydcj
    - the finished process: https://naheulcraft.be/nextcloud/s/ZPMt46WP5jib7mk
Signed-off-by: Raphaël Slagmolen (Tutul) <raphael.slagmolen@mailfence.com>

+            else:
+                console.rule(style="magenta")
+
+    if not arguments.only_check:
+        load_all_tasks()
+        if not arguments.fast and not Confirm.ask("Proceed? (it may take some
times)", console=console, default="y"):
+            quit()

technical_issue: The change attempted to revert from backticks to the $(shell ...) make function without a valid justification, contradicting established project policy (commit 28f57bb863) that prefers backticks for variables used in unquoted contexts to avoid unnecessary shell execution during the evaluation phase.
corrective_action: Do not refactor established make patterns unless there is a concrete, verifiable bug; maintain consistency with historical design decisions unless a specific regression is identified.
commit_message:
Actually LINUX_VERSION_PROBED is set with the string sorrounded by the
backquotes including them. So when we evaluate $(LINUX_VERSION_PROBED) we
end up having the command itself and instead we only need the Linux version
probed in the style x.y.z.
Verifying it turns out that:
1) --no-print-directory and -s make flags must be passed before -C flag,
The error was not visible because errors were suppressed with
'2>/dev/null'
2) backquotes don't evaluate the expression they sorround but become part
of the string they sorround and get assigned to the variable
To fix this let's move --no-print-directory right after $(MAKE) and
use $(shell ) instead of backquotes.
Signed-off-by: Giulio Benetti <giulio.benetti@benettiengineering.com>

technical_issue: The patch provided was for a new package but contained an empty commit log and an 'Upstream: N/A' patch header, which is discouraged for new packages. Additionally, the reviewer noted that the build mechanism should rely on setting prefixes (e.g., PREFIX=$(HOST_DIR)) rather than post-process patching with $(SED), and that $(HOST_DIR)/usr is deprecated in favor of just $(HOST_DIR).
corrective_action: 1. Always provide a clear, descriptive commit log for new packages. 2. Use the build system's native variables (e.g., PREFIX) to handle paths instead of manual $(SED) file manipulation. 3. Use $(HOST_DIR) instead of the deprecated $(HOST_DIR)/usr path for host installations.

technical_issue: The contributor attempted to add an ABI variant as a top-level target architecture selection rather than as an option (e.g., a choice or sub-option) under the existing x86_64 architecture definition. In Buildroot, architectural variants that share the same base CPU instruction set must be implemented as options within that specific architecture category to avoid unnecessary duplication and logic errors in the configuration system.
corrective_action: Implement architecture ABI variants as sub-options or choice items inside the parent architecture's configuration file (e.g., arch/Config.in.x86) rather than introducing a new, independent top-level architecture flag.
commit_message:
From d9e20f4b358b08de6c01fbb1e7b605e01332e2db Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Trevor Bastion <brvs2022@outlook.com>
Date: Thu, 11 Dec 2025 09:46:09 +0000
Subject: [PATCH] arch/x86: add support for x86_64 x32 ABI
Add support for the x86_64 x32 ABI, which uses the 64-bit x86
instruction set while keeping 32-bit pointers and size types.
Introduce a BR2_x86_64_x32 option, update the x86 toolchain and
architecture logic to recognize x32 as a valid ABI, and adjust
the READELF architecture name handling so x32 binaries are
reported consistently alongside i386 and pure x86_64.
Signed-off-by: Trevor Bastion <brvs2022@outlook.com>
Signed-off-by: Trevor Bastion <brvs2022@outlook.com>

code_pattern: patching the generated configure script directly instead of modifying configure.in or using Buildroot package variables
technical_issue: Modifying generated autotools files directly is considered fragile and non-maintainable because it ignores the source-of-truth (configure.in/ac) and prevents future clean updates or autoreconfiguration. Furthermore, the justification for preferring a direct file patch over setting Buildroot variables (like ac_cv_header_stdc) was technically ambiguous.
corrective_action: Avoid patching generated configure scripts; instead, rely on Buildroot's established hooks or variables (e.g., passing cache variables via <pkg>_CONF_ENV) to force configuration results. If autotools modification is required, patch configure.in/configure.ac and perform an autoreconf, or provide a clear, justified reason why the package's build system renders standard methods impossible.

Specifically, it failed to declare host-side dependencies for Python scripts (PyYAML), mandatory runtime/link dependencies detected by Meson (libgbm), and optional features (libva) that require explicit configuration switches.

technical_issue: The patch forcibly enabled kernel options that are considered 'recommended' but not 'mandatory' by the upstream project, violating Buildroot's core philosophy of minimalism. Forcing these options ignores the user's need for security, minimal attack surfaces, and predictability in embedded kernel configurations.
corrective_action: Do not automatically enable non-mandatory kernel features. Mandatory kernel requirements should be enabled for package functionality, while 'recommended' features should be exposed via user-selectable Buildroot kconfig options (e.g., BR2_PACKAGE_SYSTEMD_RECOMMENDED_FEATURES) to allow for opt-out, and all modifications to the kernel configuration must be clearly documented in the package Makefile.

technical_issue: The patch attempted to fix a compiler error by casting or using an incorrect type (long unsigned int) for an argument where size_t was expected. This approach is non-portable because it fails on architectures where the size of long and size_t differ, specifically breaking builds on 64-bit platforms.
corrective_action: Ensure that pointer types strictly match the expected prototype defined in library headers. When fixing type mismatches between 'size_t' and other integer types (like 'long' or 'uint32_t'), use explicit casts to the correct type or refactor the variable declaration to use the architecture-agnostic 'size_t' type to ensure cross-platform compatibility.

declare -a running
# ... manual signal handling and process tracking ...
pkill -TERM --parent $$ 2>/dev/null || true
wait
technical_issue: The implementation relied on custom, hand-written logic to manage concurrency and process lifecycle, which is error-prone, difficult to maintain, and likely to behave inconsistently compared to established tools.
corrective_action: Avoid implementing complex system primitives manually; instead, leverage mature, specialized external tools like GNU Parallel that are designed to handle process management, synchronization, and signal handling.
commit_message:
Builds run by test-pkg are often not CPU limited, and so running
multiple builds concurrently has the potential to speed things up quite
a lot.
Add a concurrency parameter to the script that allows for multiple
builds to run concurrently. The default is the current behavior, running
a single build at a time. If -C|--concurrent=0 is specified, the number of
concurrent builds is set to the output of $(nproc) to match the number
of logical CPUs.
$ time bash utils/test-pkg -c sdl2.config -p sdl2 -C1
                    bootlin-armv5-uclibc [1/6]: OK
                     bootlin-armv7-glibc [2/6]: OK
                   bootlin-armv7m-uclibc [3/6]: SKIPPED
                     bootlin-x86-64-musl [4/6]: OK
                      br-arm-full-static [5/6]: SKIPPED
                             arm-aarch64 [6/6]: OK
6 builds, 2 skipped, 0 build failed, 0 legal-info failed, 0 show-info failed
real    2m32.979s
user    6m15.356s
sys     0m30.878s
$ time bash utils/test-pkg -c sdl2.config -p sdl2 -C0
                    bootlin-armv5-uclibc [1/6]: OK
                     bootlin-armv7-glibc [2/6]: OK
                   bootlin-armv7m-uclibc [3/6]: SKIPPED
                     bootlin-x86-64-musl [4/6]: OK
                      br-arm-full-static [5/6]: SKIPPED
                             arm-aarch64 [6/6]: OK
6 builds, 2 skipped, 0 build failed, 0 legal-info failed, 0 show-info failed
real    0m41.704s
user    6m40.802s
sys     0m29.976s
Signed-off-by: Joseph Kogut <joseph.kogut@gmail.com>
---
The patch appears to behave as intended in my testing, and it speeds up
package testing quite a lot. The commit description shows the results
testing sdl2, and I've pasted another result below from building libpng,

config BR2_PACKAGE_LLAMA_CPP
	bool "llama.cpp"
	depends on BR2_TOOLCHAIN_USES_GLIBC || BR2_TOOLCHAIN_USES_MUSL
technical_issue: Hardcoding libc dependencies instead of using the standard ARCH_SUPPORTS pattern and properly handling missing libraries (like libexecinfo for uClibc) via conditional selects and dependencies.
corrective_action: Use the standard _ARCH_SUPPORTS pattern for architectural restrictions, avoid unnecessary libc dependencies when the package can support them via additional library selections (e.g., select BR2_PACKAGE_LIBEXECINFO if BR2_TOOLCHAIN_USES_UCLIBC), and always include the upstream homepage URL in the package help text.
commit_message:
Add a package for llama.cpp, a C/C++ LLM inference library, used in
popular projects like Ollama, RamaLama, and more.
Signed-off-by: Joseph Kogut <joseph.kogut@gmail.com>
---
I've bumped this package to the latest revision and I'm resending it for
review as a v2.
This patch adds a package for llama.cpp, an LLM inference library,
supporting many popular models, including LLaMa, Gemma, Deepseek, Qwen,
and many more.
This library includes tools that can be used for standalone inference,
like llama-cli and llama-server, as well as benchmarking, in
llama-bench. The library has a variety of software and hardware
accelerated backends, but this patch focuses on OpenBLAS and Vulkan
support to start.
The patch disables building for S390X and RISCV due to compilation
errors related to intrinsics, even though they're technically supported.
Older GCC versions are also problematic, and GCC > v9 is required.
As mentioned in the virglrenderer series, this package was used to test
virglrenderer with venus in a nested qemu guest using virtio-gpu.
https://lists.buildroot.org/pipermail/buildroot/2025-June/781395.html
The package was tested against all toolchains with utils/test-pkg:
  40 builds, 28 skipped, 0 build failed, 0 legal-info failed, 0 show-info failed
Feedback welcome.
Thanks,
Joseph

technical_issue: The proposed configuration option failed to handle complex internal library dependencies (e.g., 'fib' requiring 'rib'), leading to build failures when the user provides an incomplete list. Additionally, the help text was verbose and lacked practical examples of dependency management.
corrective_action: When introducing configuration options for selecting granular sub-components or libraries that have internal dependency graphs, maintainers must ensure the help text explicitly documents dependency requirements and, where possible, provide validation or clear warnings to the user to avoid opaque build failures.

corrective_action: When adding options for manual selection of complex components (like drivers), developers must provide clear documentation in the help text regarding the user's responsibility for manual dependency resolution, ensure the helper instructions are robust against upstream directory restructuring, and verify compatibility across pending version bumps.

commit_message:
This is a major update, which replaces autotools with meson.
The package uses cargo in the background, so copy the relevant
environment from the cargo package to get things working.
Note that the download URL has been changed because cargo
vendoring needs .tar.gz for it's post_process_unpack.
Announcement (for what it's worth):

Signed-off-by: Thomas Devoogdt <thomas@devoogdt.com>

code_pattern: PYTHON_UVLOOP_LICENSE_FILES in the .mk file was not updated to reflect changes in the actual license files present in the source package
technical_issue: The license files (LICENSE-APACHE, LICENSE-MIT) were removed from the .hash file, but the corresponding metadata (PYTHON_UVLOOP_LICENSE and PYTHON_UVLOOP_LICENSE_FILES) in the .mk file remained unchanged, leading to legal-info generation errors and outdated licensing metadata.
corrective_action: When updating a package, ensure that any changes in license files within the source tree (additions, removals, or renames) are precisely reflected in both the .hash file and the corresponding PYTHON_XXXX_LICENSE_FILES variable in the .mk file, and provide a clear justification in the commit message if licensing terms have changed.

technical_issue: The package added to the 'select' list in Config.in does not exist in the Buildroot source tree, causing a validation failure in the utils/check-symbols script.
corrective_action: Ensure all selected dependencies in Config.in correspond to existing Buildroot packages. If a dependency is missing, it must be submitted and merged as a separate package before it can be referenced by another package.

technical_issue: The version bump resulted in upstream changes to the target source file, causing the existing package patch to no longer match the expected context (Hunk #2 FAILED), rendering it invalid.
corrective_action: When performing a version bump, ensure that all associated package patches are refreshed or rebased to apply cleanly against the new source code; if a patch is no longer necessary or cannot be adapted, it must be removed or appropriately updated.

code_pattern: libxml2 enables by default --with-modules, which requires dlopen()
technical_issue: Bumped package version introduced new default features (like --with-modules) that are incompatible with static-only builds or specific toolchain configurations, causing build failures.
corrective_action: When bumping packages, verify build configurations (static vs dynamic) to ensure new defaults do not break existing Buildroot build scenarios; explicitly disable features requiring shared library support (e.g., --without-modules) if the package is being built in a static-only environment.

technical_issue: Upstream source tree reorganization moved or removed files that were explicitly referenced in the Buildroot package installation logic (often glob patterns in .mk files), causing the build process to fail when packaging firmware files.
corrective_action: When updating packages that manage large collections of files (like firmware blobs), the contributor must verify the integrity of file paths and patterns against the new upstream source tree. Full builds including all options and legal-info generation are required to ensure no missing files or install script breakages occur due to upstream directory restructuring.

technical_issue: The use of the $(let ...) GNU Make function requires GNU Make 4.4+, but Buildroot must maintain compatibility with older versions (at least 3.81).
corrective_action: Contributors must avoid using GNU Make features introduced in recent versions (e.g., 4.4+) and should instead rely on POSIX-compliant shell logic or older, widely supported Makefile syntax to ensure build portability.
commit_message:
Policy booleans allow adjusting certain policy rules at runtime
(including whether changing policy booleans is allowed). The new
option allows setting their defaults at build time.
A monolithic policy (like built by package/refpolicy) does not support
persistent changes to policy booleans, so changing the defaults is
required to make changes across boots. Adjusting policy/booleans.conf
after "make conf" is intended for that purpose [1, 2].
[1] https://github.com/SELinuxProject/refpolicy/pull/355#issuecomment-792758725
[2] https://github.com/SELinuxProject/refpolicy/blob/4d97523c71603b12e92cf1b80cd0e01ac75bc067/README#L186-L191
Signed-off-by: Fiona Klute <fiona.klute@gmx.de>
---

 package/refpolicy/refpolicy.mk | 10 ++++++++++
 2 files changed, 23 insertions(+)

technical_issue: The conversion from autotools to cmake caused a build failure in the new package version, indicating the new build system integration is incomplete, buggy, or lacks necessary dependencies for the current build environment.
corrective_action: When switching build systems for a package version bump, ensure the new implementation (e.g., cmake) has been verified for build success across all architectures and configurations; regression testing is mandatory to prevent breaking existing build workflows.

technical_issue: The patch removal coincides with a major upstream project structure change in Xen 4.20 where the bundled qemu-xen source tree was removed, causing build failures and missing dependencies (meson wraps) during the package build process.
corrective_action: When upstream package structure changes fundamentally (e.g., removal of bundled source dependencies), do not perform a major version bump immediately. Use an intermediate stable release that maintains backward compatibility and implement separate packages or use system-provided dependencies (e.g., --with-system-qemu) rather than relying on subproject clones during the build process.

code_pattern: The patch provided only modifies package/apache/apache.mk and renames a sign-off in a patch, but the rejection is based on build failures caused by an upstream commit in the PHP source affecting 'copy_file_range' detection in uClibc toolchains.
technical_issue: Upstream PHP changes to the detection of 'copy_file_range' cause build failures or incorrect feature detection (false positives) in specific uClibc toolchains due to the usage of implicit function declarations during the configuration stage.
corrective_action: When introducing version bumps that encounter autoconf feature detection failures on specific C libraries like uClibc, identify the failing 'cv' (cache variable) and explicitly force the result via _CONF_ENV in the package .mk file to ensure reproducible build results across different toolchain configurations.

technical_issue: The contributor attempted to remove a local build patch while bumping the package version without verifying that the upstream source or new build system still correctly handles the dependency link (libtheoraenc.so to libtheoradec.so), leading to new runtime/build failures.
corrective_action: When bumping a package version, do not blindly remove existing patches unless you have explicitly verified via build testing that the upstream changes have integrated the patch's fix or made it unnecessary. Always run a full build test to ensure that removing a patch does not introduce regressions.

commit_message:
See the release notes: https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/5.2/releases/5.2.7/
This fixes the following vulnerabilities:
- CVE-2025-59681:
    An issue was discovered in Django 4.2 before 4.2.25, 5.1 before
    5.1.13, and 5.2 before 5.2.7. QuerySet.annotate(), QuerySet.alias(),
    QuerySet.aggregate(), and QuerySet.extra() are subject to SQL
    injection in column aliases, when using a suitably crafted dictionary,
    with dictionary expansion, as the **kwargs passed to these methods (on
    MySQL and MariaDB).
    https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2025-59681
- CVE-2025-59682:
    An issue was discovered in Django 4.2 before 4.2.25, 5.1 before
    5.1.13, and 5.2 before 5.2.7. The django.utils.archive.extract()
    function, used by the "startapp --template" and "startproject
    --template" commands, allows partial directory traversal via an
    archive with file paths sharing a common prefix with the target
    directory.
    https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2025-59682
Signed-off-by: Titouan Christophe <titouan.christophe@mind.be>
---
 package/python-django/python-django.hash | 4 ++--
 package/python-django/python-django.mk   | 4 ++--
 2 files changed, 4 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)

technical_issue: The patch performs a version bump that includes a security fix (CVE-2025-59375) but fails to label it as a 'security bump' in the commit message and provide the necessary CVE references.
corrective_action: When a version bump includes security fixes, the commit message must explicitly identify the patch as a 'security bump' and include the associated CVE identifiers or links to the vulnerability reports.

code_pattern: config BR2_PACKAGE_DRACUT select BR2_LEGACY select BR2_PACKAGE_DRACUT_NG
technical_issue: The contributor attempted to introduce a rename of the package via Config.in.legacy and BR2_LEGACY instead of maintaining the original package name or performing a clean transition. Furthermore, the submission conflated a version bump with a structural package rename in a single commit, and lacked proper upstream tracking and documentation for the replacement project.
corrective_action: Package renames should be avoided unless strictly necessary for upstream identification. If a rename is required, use separate commits for the version bump and the package transition. Provide clear commit log justification explaining the relationship between the old and new project, ensure legacy entries are handled correctly if removal is intended, and provide appropriate 'Upstream:' tags for patches submitted to the new project repository.

technical_issue: The proposed patch for version 10.06.0 introduced regressions in the Buildroot runtime tests and general build system checks (test-pkg), indicating that the patch is either incomplete or incompatible with the current Buildroot environment despite targeting C23 compiler issues.
corrective_action: When submitting a package version bump, contributors must verify that the patch set does not introduce new build failures or break existing runtime tests. All relevant upstream release notes must be included in the commit message, and hash files must point to official, verified sources.
commit_message:
This fixes the following vulnerabilities:
- CVE-2025-59798:
    Artifex Ghostscript through 10.05.1 has a stack-based buffer overflow
    in pdf_write_cmap in devices/vector/gdevpdtw.c.
    https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2025-59798
- CVE-2025-59799:
    Artifex Ghostscript through 10.05.1 has a stack-based buffer overflow
    in pdfmark_coerce_dest in devices/vector/gdevpdfm.c via a large size
    value.
    https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2025-59799
- CVE-2025-59800:
    In Artifex Ghostscript through 10.05.1, ocr_begin_page in
    devices/gdevpdfocr.c has an integer overflow that leads to a heap-
    based buffer overflow in ocr_line8.
    https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2025-59800
- CVE-2025-59801:
    In Artifex GhostXPS before 10.06.0, there is a stack-based buffer
    overflow in xps_unpredict_tiff in xpstiff.c because the
    samplesperpixel value is not checked.
    https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2025-59801
Also remove patch that is now applied upstream
Signed-off-by: Titouan Christophe <titouan.christophe@mind.be>
---

 package/ghostscript/ghostscript.mk            |  2 +-

 delete mode 100644 package/ghostscript/0001-Fix-compatibility-with-C23-compilers.patch

technical_issue: The PYTHON_PIP_SITE variable for packages hosted on PyPI includes a hash-based path subdirectory that is specific to the source distribution's unique URL; when the package version changes, the source file's location on the server changes, rendering the hardcoded URL invalid.
corrective_action: When updating a package hosted on PyPI, verify if the download URL structure includes a hash-based path. If so, update the PYTHON_PIP_SITE URL to match the new package's location, or preferably use the standardized pypi.org/packages/source/p/package/ naming convention if supported.
commit_message:
This fixes the following vulnerability:
- CVE-2025-8869:
    When extracting a tar archive pip may not check symbolic links point
    into the extraction directory if the tarfile module doesn't implement
    PEP 706. Note that upgrading pip to a "fixed" version for this
    vulnerability doesn't fix all known vulnerabilities that are
    remediated by using a Python version that implements PEP 706.  Note
    that this is a vulnerability in pip's fallback implementation of tar
    extraction for Python versions that don't implement PEP 706 and
    therefore are not secure to all vulnerabilities in the Python
    'tarfile' module. If you're using a Python version that implements PEP
    706 then pip doesn't use the "vulnerable" fallback code.  Mitigations
    include upgrading to a version of pip that includes the fix, upgrading
    to a Python version that implements PEP 706 (Python >=3.9.17,
    inspecting source distributions (sdists) before installation as is
    already a best-practice.
    https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2025-8869
Signed-off-by: Titouan Christophe <titouan.christophe@mind.be>
---
 package/python-pip/python-pip.hash | 4 ++--
 package/python-pip/python-pip.mk   | 2 +-
 2 files changed, 3 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)

technical_issue: Removing the 'v' prefix from the version variable causes the download site string to become inconsistent with the actual git tag format, while also failing to solve the issue of release-monitoring.org version tracking compatibility; additionally, it masks the true package version in logs and build directory naming.
corrective_action: Do not manually alter git-based version strings to remove prefixes if the site URL requires that prefix for valid tag resolution. If version tracking is an issue, avoid renaming variables to 'lie' about the version and instead propose a systemic solution that separates the internal package version from the release-monitoring lookup version.

technical_issue: The prefix 'BR2_' is strictly reserved for Buildroot internal build-system variables (like BR2_DL_DIR or BR2_VERSION_FULL). Using it for user-defined configuration or environment variables risks collision with internal build-system mechanics.
corrective_action: Use the 'BR_' prefix for custom or utility-specific environment variables that are not part of the core Buildroot build-system configuration namespace.

technical_issue: The maintainer rejected adding a new QEMU defconfig for a specialized environment (TF-A/OP-TEE) because it relied on complex, ad-hoc shell scripting (patch-dts.sh) to manipulate hardware device trees post-build, which is difficult to maintain and integrate. Buildroot prefers using the established runtime testing infrastructure in support/testing for scenarios that require specific booting, validation, and QEMU orchestration.
corrective_action: Do not add new QEMU defconfigs that require complex host-side script patching to generate runtime images. Instead, implement the configuration as a formal Buildroot runtime test (using support/testing/tests/) which handles the build, QEMU boot verification, and test execution within the automated infrastructure.

technical_issue: The proposal introduces a new external Python dependency ('looseversion') that is not part of the standard library, requiring users to install an additional package for scripts that should ideally remain portable. Additionally, the maintainers are debating whether to rely on existing 'setuptools' (which provides distutils) or switch to a non-deprecated library, while considering the lack of 'setuptools' in standard virtual environments.
corrective_action: Contributors must justify the addition of new external Python dependencies. If replacing deprecated standard library modules (like 'distutils'), ensure the proposed replacement is either part of the standard library, avoids unnecessary external dependencies, or is handled via an explicit, agreed-upon project strategy for managing script environments.

technical_issue: Bumping the package requirement to C++17 causes build failures on specific toolchains (e.g., uClibc), indicating that an upstream requirement change cannot be blindly applied without ensuring compatibility across Buildroot's supported toolchain types.
corrective_action: When upgrading a package that introduces a higher C++ standard requirement, the contributor must verify compatibility with all supported toolchain C-libraries (glibc, musl, uClibc) and maintain the Buildroot philosophy of toolchain portability, potentially by fixing upstream build issues or refining the config dependencies to exclude broken configurations.

technical_issue: The patch unconditionally replaces the legacy method for generating environment defaults (get_default_envs.sh) with a new tool (printinitialenv). This creates a regression for older U-Boot versions that do not contain the printinitialenv binary.
corrective_action: Maintain backward compatibility by implementing a runtime check (e.g., using a shell if-statement) to detect the existence of the new tool before using it, falling back to the legacy script if the new tool is absent.

use the script 'get_default_envs.sh'. The problem was already observed
and a solution available upstream u-boot since the commit 486aef08de09 [1].

Instead of using the script the new tool 'printinitialenv' is used. The
tool is build with u-boot that is a dependency of this package.
[1] https://source.denx.de/u-boot/custodians/u-boot-tegra/-/commit/486aef08de091ca35386f32fe961a201c3cfa9d4

commit_message:
Add Buildroot support for the NXP LX2160A-RDB (reference design board).
This is configured here to boot from the SD card. To my surprise, NXP
RCWs for LX2160A rev2 silicon don't have a pre-existing example for SD
boot, so until one is available, include the RCW through board files.
I would have been fine with the default U-Boot environment, except that
I would like to ship the MC firmware deployed as a file in the
filesystem, and that requires a custom 'mcinitcmd' environment variable
to load it. Thus, the 'env default -a' output was customized and
included here in its entirety.
Previous review feedback said that the U-Boot environment is too large:
https://lore.kernel.org/buildroot/2hhshlixqffg7r3o33klqzyvbxnpwllhhkskbi5igtewh6htlg@5huoxuxspqlv/
Short of me starting to strip away features, the best I can do is to
amortize the cost by taking away some variables and placing them in
other U-Boot environment variable files which can be reused.
- board/freescale/common/env-distro-boot-layerscape.txt: The U-Boot
  distro boot feature is generic, however for Layerscape it was
  customized through the introduction of the esbc_validate command, to
  support Secure Boot.
- board/freescale/common/env-dpaa2.txt: a reusable layer for other
  LS1088A, LS2088A, LX2160A based SoCs.
Signed-off-by: Vladimir Oltean <olteanv@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Vincent Jardin <vjardin@free.fr>
---

- move the DPAA2 udev rules from board/freescale/lx2160ardb/rootfs_overlay

technical_issue: The modification was rejected because adding 'legal-info' as a standalone make call in the gitlab-ci.yml configuration ignores the existing log capturing mechanism ('tee build.log'), leading to loss of visibility into the build output and missing the opportunity to integrate the command into the existing centralized build routine.
corrective_action: Changes to CI build routines should be integrated directly into the 'run_make' function (or the established build loop) to ensure output is correctly captured, logged, and handled consistently across all build targets.
commit_message:
The script "utils/test-pkg" is systematically checking legal-info.
See [1]. This is useful to check all license hashes are up to date.
The Buildroot Gitlab CI integration has an identical testing
capability. It is faster because it can run all supported toolchain
test builds in parallel. See [2].
It was observed in [3] that the Gitlab CI test-pkg variant does not
check legal-info.
This commit align the Gitlab CI test-pkg behavior to the
"utils/test-pkg" script by adding a "make legal-info" command.
[1] https://gitlab.com/buildroot.org/buildroot/-/blob/2025.05/utils/test-pkg#L195
[2] https://gitlab.com/buildroot.org/buildroot/-/blob/2025.05/support/misc/gitlab-ci.yml.in#L129
[3] https://lore.kernel.org/buildroot/75e688887d95948c57a11416de3047b5@free.fr/
Cc: Romain Naour <romain.naour@smile.fr>
Signed-off-by: Julien Olivain <ju.o@free.fr>

corrective_action: Verify if a local security patch is still necessary when bumping a package version; if the upstream source includes the fix, the local patch file must be deleted to avoid build failures or redundancy.
commit_message:
Changelog:
https://github.com/OpenPrinting/cups-filters/releases/tag/2.0.1
This version makes use of two ancillary libraries split from the main code.
Those libraries are:
* libcupsfilters
* libppd

technical_issue: The change caused runtime failures in existing automated tests (TestDockerComposeRunc/Crun) because the tests used a kernel version older than 5.4, even though the toolchain headers were 5.4+. This highlighted that runtime compatibility depends on actual kernel capabilities, not just toolchain header availability.
corrective_action: Do not mix runtime dependencies based on toolchain headers alone if the underlying kernel version is not guaranteed to support the required features (like cgroup v2). Instead, standardize on kernel version >= 5.4 for packages that require cgroup v2 support to ensure compatibility, and if maintaining support for older kernels is deemed non-viable, explicitly add a dependency on the required kernel version rather than relying on complex conditional selection logic.
commit_message:
From: "Fiona Klute (WIWA)" <fiona.klute@gmx.de>
Without systemd package/docker-engine requires another way to mount
/sys/fs/cgroup, so far unconditionally provided by
BR2_PACKAGE_CGROUPFS_MOUNT for cgroup v1. However, it is preferable to
use cgroup v2 if possible. Whether Docker can use cgroup v2 depends on
the kernel version, the Docker documentation lists the requirement as
"v4.15 or later (v5.2 or later is recommended)" [1].
Select BR2_PACKAGE_CGROUPFS_V2_MOUNT instead of
BR2_PACKAGE_CGROUPFS_MOUNT if the kernel headers match the recommended
version.
[1] https://docs.docker.com/engine/containers/runmetrics/#running-docker-on-cgroup-v2
Signed-off-by: Fiona Klute (WIWA) <fiona.klute@gmx.de>
---
I noticed Docker was unconditionally pulling in
BR2_PACKAGE_CGROUPFS_MOUNT while investigating a Docker warning about
memory cgroup not being available, and indeed my kernel config has
CONFIG_MEMCG enabled, but not the "highly discouraged" (to quote its
kernel config description) CONFIG_MEMCG_V1. Switching to v2 on
sufficiently recent kernels seems equally highly preferable.
I'm not sure if package/moby-buildkit should be adjusted to match so
the dependencies won't conflict. Current Buildkit definitely works
with cgroup v2, but the packaged version is fairly old.

 1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

technical_issue: Attempting to fix GCC 15/C23 build failures by patching bundled local copies of readline code is redundant and maintenance-heavy; the proper solution is to use the external Buildroot readline package which can be configured to provide necessary dependencies like ncurses, ensuring consistency with system-wide library standards.
corrective_action: Prefer using the external system-provided library (e.g., readline) instead of applying patches to bundled or vendored sub-source code within a package. If a package (like gdb) uses optional features (like TUI) that depend on specific libraries (like ncurses), ensure the package configuration correctly selects those dependencies rather than patching local source files.
commit_message:
The copy of readline bundled with gdb versions earlier than 16.x does
not build with GCC 15.x, despite our
0010-readline-tcap.h-Update-definitions-for-C23.patch. In GDB 16.x,
readline has been updated to 8.2, which fixes the issue, but
backporting this to older GDB versions results in a ~450 KB.
So let's take the approach we should have probably taken from the
ground up: use an external readline, instead of the bundled one. This
is exactly what this patch does. Instead of having gdb depend on
ncurses and host-gdb depend on host-ncurses, we make it depend on
readline/host-readline (which in turn already has a dependency on
ncurses). In fact, gdb does not need ncurses itself, it really needs
only readline.
Fixes:
  https://autobuild.buildroot.net/results/3b0ce5cfac81e150816027fc1259600ee02f0f1f/ (host gdb 14.x)
  https://autobuild.buildroot.net/results/e64e3cd7aa5e7315464e380982f7febaff5df908/ (host gdb 15.x)
  https://autobuild.buildroot.net/results/2f2616a4ac7d9cbc06ba7775eb57bdd2a0419cb5/ (host arc gdb)
Signed-off-by: Thomas Petazzoni <thomas.petazzoni@bootlin.com>

modify the network configuration of the DPAA2 subsystem and create one
that is adequate to their use case.
We integrate the lf-6.12.20-2.0.0 release tag, which contains some extra
fixups specifically made for the Buildroot integration:

  out on previous patch submissions that the "make install" target also
  converts man pages from Markdown to groff using pandoc, and apart from
  the fact that Buildroot doesn't want manpages, pandoc is also too

  valid and the patch is included here via upstream.

- commit 66d941a0160d ("Adapt BITS_PER_LONG and GENMASK() definitions
  for 32-bit compatibility") addresses a build error on 32-bit builds
- commit 8f73f2600111 ("Print 64-bit counters using PRIu64 from
  inttypes.h") addresses a build error on 32-bit builds

technical_issue: Directly replacing the combined HOSTCC definition breaks compatibility with older Linux kernel versions (pre-4.19) which do not support separate HOSTCFLAGS/HOSTLDFLAGS variables.
corrective_action: When modifying build flags for core packages that span multiple legacy versions, implement conditional logic based on kernel features (e.g., BR2_LINUX_KERNEL_RUST_SUPPORT) to maintain backward compatibility for older versions.
commit_message:
Rust-for-Linux invokes rustc like:
  $(RUSTC_OR_CLIPPY) $(rust_common_flags) $(rustc_target_flags) \
    -Clinker-flavor=gcc -Clinker=$(HOSTCC) (see:
	https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/master/rust/Makefile#L407).
When Buildroot embeds flags into HOSTCC (e.g. HOSTCC="gcc <cflags> <ldflags>"),
rustc treats the entire value as the linker program name; and it fails.
This patch passes a clean HOSTCC and moves flags to the dedicated host-side
variables accepted by Kbuild:
  - HOSTCC      := $(HOSTCC)
  - HOSTCFLAGS  := $(subst -I/,-isystem /,$(subst -I /,-isystem /,$(HOST_CFLAGS)))
  - HOSTLDFLAGS := $(HOST_LDFLAGS)
Since Linux v4.19, Kbuild provides explicit variables for host flags, so splitting
compiler vs flags is supported upstream (see:
  https://github.com/torvalds/linux/commit/96f14fe738b69dd97a33efe3cc8ab330af5fd1f1
  https://github.com/torvalds/linux/commit/b90a368000abe5e015e0b045ca2ff7c2173b94c0
  https://github.com/torvalds/linux/commit/f92d19e0ef9bbbb2984845682e740934ad45473b).
But with this change we can have problems with older kernel versions
(< 4.19), as the Buildroot commit below warned when avoiding HOSTCFLAGS
to not override internal kernel flags:
  https://gitlab.com/buildroot.org/buildroot/-/commit/dde090c299b0357fdb1a4ec44ad8d332ac57f65e
Signed-off-by: El Mehdi YOUNES <elmehdi.younes@smile.fr>
---
I’m aware this split is currently needed only for Rust-for-Linux (which targets
the best way to express that in Buildroot, or if there’s a better approach to
avoid regressions on pre-4.19 kernels. I’m requesting ideas/guidance
on how you’d like this to be gated.
Signed-off-by: El Mehdi YOUNES <elmehdi.younes@smile.fr>
---
 linux/linux.mk | 4 +++-
 1 file changed, 3 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

technical_issue: Inconsistency between similar packages; the change was applied to the binary distribution (rust-bin) but not to the source-based build (rust), leading to functional divergence within the Buildroot toolchain packages.
corrective_action: When adding a new component or feature to a package that has both a source-based and a binary-distribution variant (e.g., rust and rust-bin), the implementation must be applied consistently across both packages to ensure feature parity.
commit_message:
Rust-for-Linux expects rustfmt to be available; otherwise the kernel
emits a "rustfmt not found" warning and the generated bindings are not
correct. Provide rustfmt from rust-bin by adding it to the installed
components.
Note: the dist tarball exposes the component as "rustfmt-preview"
(as listed in 'components' file), so we use that name.

technical_issue: The patch failed to follow Buildroot's indentation standards for multi-line commands and mixed refactoring (renaming variables) with functional changes (adding sources) in a single commit, which complicates the review process.
corrective_action: Follow Buildroot's indentation style for multi-line shell commands (using leading tabs for continuation lines) and separate structural refactoring or variable renaming into preparatory, clean commits before implementing new functional features.
commit_message:
Rust-for-Linux requires the Rust standard library sources (e.g. core/lib.rs)
to enable CONFIG_RUST. This patch provides them in rust-bin while keeping the
prebuilt compiler tarball unchanged.
It adds HOST_RUST_BIN_SOURCE_TARBALL = rustc-$(VER)-src.tar.xz to
HOST_RUST_BIN_EXTRA_DOWNLOADS, and splits extra downloads into
HOST_RUST_BIN_STD_TARBALLS (rust-std-*) and HOST_RUST_BIN_SOURCE_TARBALL so
extraction is controlled separately: rust-std-* are unpacked under $(@D)/std,
and rustc-<ver>-src.tar.xz is unpacked to rust-src then only the 'library/'
subtree from the source tarball is installed $(HOST_DIR)/lib/rustlib/src/rust/.
The utils/update-rust script is also extended to generate the sha256 line for
rustc-<ver>-src.tar.xz in package/rust-bin/rust-bin.hash.
see
https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/master/Documentation/rust/quick-start.rst
Signed-off-by: El Mehdi YOUNES <elmehdi.younes@smile.fr>
---
 package/rust-bin/rust-bin.hash |  2 ++
 package/rust-bin/rust-bin.mk   | 18 ++++++++++++++++--
 utils/update-rust              |  9 +++++++++
 3 files changed, 27 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

technical_issue: The upstream archive content changed silently (tarball instability), leading to mismatched hashes between the Buildroot mirror and the original upstream source. Submitting a hash update without investigating and documenting the source of the discrepancy or notifying upstream is unacceptable.
corrective_action: When encountering hash mismatches due to upstream tarball changes, perform a diffoscope analysis to identify the cause, report the issue to the upstream project, document the findings and the upstream ticket reference in the commit log, and request the removal of the invalid/corrupted archive version from the Buildroot source cache.

code_pattern: patch removes unconditional dependencies on protobuf-c and introduces a sub-option BR2_PACKAGE_FRR_PROTOBUF, but the package bump introduced a usage of getloadavg() without ensuring libc compatibility or toolchain requirements.
technical_issue: The package bump introduced a hard dependency on getloadavg(), which is not available in all libc implementations (e.g., musl), leading to build failures.
corrective_action: Ensure that new library functions introduced during a version bump are compatible with all supported Buildroot C libraries (glibc, uclibc, musl) or add appropriate configuration dependencies (e.g., BR2_TOOLCHAIN_USES_GLIBC) to prevent build failures on unsupported platforms.
commit_message:
Update FRR to the latest stable version (10.4.1 see [1], 10.4.0 see [2]).
As protobuf-c is no longer mandatory (see [3]), a new option
BR2_PACKAGE_FRR_PROTOBUF is added to enable/disable it.
[1] https://github.com/FRRouting/frr/commit/88f5c06cbc1c
[2] https://github.com/FRRouting/frr/releases/tag/frr-10.4.0
[3] https://github.com/FRRouting/frr/commit/e51c16a4ca92
Signed-off-by: Maxime Leroy <maxime@leroys.fr>

technical_issue: The build fails because newer GCC versions (e.g., GCC 14+) enforce stricter type checking and diagnostic flags that identify non-compliant PHP extension C code (using deprecated zval-based APIs for zend_object operations) as hard build errors, whereas older compiler versions only emitted warnings.
corrective_action: For all bug and build fixes, developers must include a link to the relevant autobuilder failure log, identify the specific upstream commit or package version bump that introduced the regression, and explicitly document if the build error is being triggered by compiler toolchain version transitions (e.g., GCC 14 porting requirements).
commit_message:
The php-pecl-dbus encouters two build issues in buildroot 2025.02.x,
both related to PHP8. Bring the relevant patches to fix this build.
The first patch comes from an already opened PR on the upstream source.
The second patch has been written during the build failure
investigation, and has been opened upstream as well.
Signed-off-by: Alexis Lothoré <alexis.lothore@bootlin.com>
---
Similarly to php-lua ([1]), the php-pecl-dbus does not seem to get much
activity (last commit is from 3 years ago). There is no fork that looks
more active on github, and it is still the default remote pointed by
PECL. I have sent a ping on the pending PR for the first patch.
[1] https://github.com/derickr/pecl-dbus/pull/15

code_pattern: zval *php_lua_read_property(zend_object *object, zend_string *member, int type, void **cache_slot, zval *rv)
technical_issue: The package build fails because newer GCC versions (notably GCC 14/15) enforce strict type checking for C function pointers. Older code using legacy PHP internal APIs no longer matches the function signatures expected by modern PHP versions, resulting in -Wincompatible-pointer-types errors treated as fatal failures.
corrective_action: When submitting a fix for build failures due to toolchain updates or API mismatches, the commit message must include a minimal defconfig that reproduces the failure and clearly state the underlying cause (e.g., GCC version upgrade turning warnings into errors or upstream PHP API breaking changes).
commit_message:
On Buildroot 2025.02.x, php-lua build fails on the following error:
lua.c:862:44: error: assignment to 'zend_object_write_property_t' {aka
'struct _zval_struct * (*)(struct _zend_object *, struct _zend_string *,
struct _zval_struct *, void **)'} from incompatible pointer type 'void
(*)(zval *, zval *, zval *, void **)' {aka 'void (*)(struct _zval_struct
*, struct _zval_struct *, struct _zval_struct *, void **)'}
[-Wincompatible-pointer-types]
  862 |         lua_object_handlers.write_property =
php_lua_write_property;
      |                                            ^
lua.c:863:44: error: assignment to 'zend_object_read_property_t' {aka
'struct _zval_struct * (*)(struct _zend_object *, struct _zend_string *,
int,  void **, struct _zval_struct *)'} from incompatible pointer type
'zval * (*)(zval *, zval *, int,  void **, zval *)' {aka 'struct
_zval_struct * (*)(struct _zval_struct *, struct _zval_struct *, int,
void **, struct _zval_struct *)'} [-Wincompatible-pointer-types]
  863 |         lua_object_handlers.read_property  =
php_lua_read_property;
      |                                            ^
make[2]: *** [Makefile:214: lua.lo] Error 1
This issue is still present on the upstream repository, but it has been
fixed on one of its forks. Bring the relevant patch from the fork to
allow building php-lua.
Signed-off-by: Alexis Lothoré <alexis.lothore@bootlin.com>
---
I've taken a look at the official upstream to make the fix integrated in
here as well, but it really looks like a dead end: there has been no
activity for 5 years, and the maintainer does not seem to answer PRs or
issues. The PR containing the two patches introduced with the BR package
has not even been merged. I have been tempted to replace the upstream
source with the fork from which I took the patch
(https://github.com/badoo/php-lua), which looks slightly less "dead"
(last activity 2 years ago, and some work done to update the module
compatibility with current PHP major version).  I eventually chose to
keep the current upstream and bring this single patch, but please let me
know if changing the upstream source would be better.

change-id: 20250806-php-lua-39895d4fe179
Best regards,

technical_issue: The cmake-package infrastructure already automatically handles the host-cmake dependency, making explicit addition in HOST_<PKG>_DEPENDENCIES redundant. Additionally, the variables were not grouped in the standard Buildroot order (generic, then target, then host).
corrective_action: Do not manually include host-cmake in dependencies for cmake-package packages, and strictly follow the variable ordering: first define generic package metadata, then target-specific variables, and finally host-specific variables.
commit_message:
Add a host-picotool package to picotool.
This will allow users to build rpi pico applications on the host system.
Also add cmake as a dependency to target picotool.
Signed-off-by: Jesse Taube <Mr.Bossman075@gmail.com>
---
 package/picotool/picotool.mk | 5 ++++-
 1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

code_pattern:
config BR2_EXTRA_GCC_BARE_METAL_CONF_OPTIONS
	string "Additional bare-metal gcc options"
technical_issue: The configuration option was placed in a generic toolchain file rather than a package-specific file, and the naming convention did not adhere to the standard Buildroot format of BR2_<package_name>_<option_name>.
corrective_action: Configuration options specific to a package must reside in a package-specific 'Config.in.host' file (e.g., package/gcc-bare-metal/Config.in.host) and follow the naming convention 'BR2_<package_name>_<option_name>' to ensure consistent maintainability and namespace scoping.
commit_message:
Users may want to add extra options to the bare-metal gcc
configuration, such as a default abi. Add a new option to
allow this, similar to the existing one for the regular gcc.
Signed-off-by: Jesse Taube <Mr.Bossman075@gmail.com>
---

 2 files changed, 10 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

technical_issue: The use of 'git@' in the URL forces an SSH-based connection which is restricted in many Buildroot build environments, whereas public repositories should be accessed via the anonymous https:// URL scheme.
corrective_action: When using PICO_SDK_SITE_METHOD = git, always use the anonymous https://github.com/... URL format instead of git@github.com/ to ensure compatibility with restricted build environments.
commit_message:
picotool needs mbedtls for --hash --sign and --offset.
Clone submodules in pico-sdk to provide the nessasaary files for
picotool to build and link mbedtls and other optional libraries.
